bleak HOUSE 
 by Charles Dickens 
 PREFACE 
 a Chancery judge once have the kindness to inform I , as one of a 
 company of some hundred and fifty man and woman not labour under

 shin subject of much popular prejudice   at which point I think 
 the judge 's eye have a cast in my direction , be almost immaculate . 
 there have be , he admit , a trivial blemish or so in its rate of 
 progress , but this be exaggerated and have be entirely owe to

 have be until lately bent in the most determined manner on by no 
 mean enlarge the number of Chancery judge appoint - I believe 
 by Richard the Second , but any other king will do as well . 
 this seem to I too profound a joke to be insert in the body of

 " my nature be subdue 
 to what it work in , like the dyer 's hand : 
 pity I , then , and wish I be renew ! " 
 but as it be wholesome that the parsimonious public should know 
 what have be do , and still be do , in this connexion , I 
 mention here that everything set forth in these page concern 
 the Court of Chancery be substantially true , and within the truth . 
 the case of Gridley be in no essential alter from one of actual 
 occurrence , make public by a disinterested person who be 
 professionally acquaint with the whole of the monstrous wrong 
 from begin to end . at the present moment   August , 1853   there 
 be a suit before the court which be commence nearly twenty year

 appear at one time , in which cost have be incur to the amount 
 of seventy thousand pound , which be a friendly SUIT , and which be 
  I be assure   no nearer to its termination now than when it be 
 begin . there be another well - know suit in Chancery , not yet 
 decide , which be commence before the close of the last century 
 and in which more than double the amount of seventy thousand pound 
 have be swallow up in cost . if I want other authority for 
 Jarndyce and Jarndyce , I could rain they on these page , to the 
 shame of - a parsimonious public . 
 there be only one other point on which I offer a word of remark . 
 the possibility of what be call spontaneous combustion have be 
 deny since the death of Mr. Krook ; and my good friend Mr. Lewes 
  quite mistaken , as he soon find , in suppose the thing to have 
 be abandon by all authority   publish some ingenious letter 
 to I at the time when that event be chronicle , argue that 
 spontaneous combustion could not possibly be . I have no need to 
 observe that I do not wilfully or negligently mislead my reader 
 and that before I write that description I take pain to 
 investigate the subject . there be about thirty case on record , 
 of which the most famous , that of the Countess Cornelia de Baudi 
 Cesenate , be minutely investigate and describe by Giuseppe 
 Bianchini , a prebendary of Verona , otherwise distinguish in 
 letter , who publish an account of it at Verona in 1731 , which he

 rational doubt , observe in that case be the appearance observe 
 in Mr. Krook 's case . the next most famous instance happen at 
 Rheims six year early , and the historian in that case be Le Cat , 
 one of the most renowned surgeon produce by France . the subject 
 be a woman , whose husband be ignorantly convict of have

 acquit because it be show upon the evidence that she have die 
 the death of which this name of spontaneous combustion be give . I 
 do not think it necessary to add to these notable fact , and that 
 general reference to the authority which will be find at page 
 30 , vol . ii . ,   the record opinion and experience of 
 distinguish medical professor , French , English , and Scotch , in 
 more modern day , content myself with observe that I shall not 
 abandon the fact until there shall have be a considerable 
 spontaneous combustion of the testimony on which human occurrence 
 be usually receive . 
 in Bleak House I have purposely dwell upon the romantic side of 
 familiar thing .

 CHAPTER I 
 in Chancery 
 London . Michaelmas term lately over , and the Lord Chancellor 
 sit in Lincoln 's Inn Hall . implacable November weather . as 
 much mud in the street as if the water have but newly retire from 
 the face of the earth , and it would not be wonderful to meet a 
 Megalosaurus , forty foot long or so , waddle like an elephantine 
 lizard up Holborn Hill . smoke lower down from chimney - pot , 
 make a soft black drizzle , with flake of soot in it as big as 
 full - grow snowflake - go into mourning , one might imagine , for 
 the death of the sun . dog , undistinguishable in mire . horse , 
 scarcely well ; splash to their very blinker . foot passenger , 
 jostle one another 's umbrella in a general infection of ill 
 temper , and lose their foot - hold at street - corner , where ten of 
 thousand of other foot passenger have be slip and slide 
 since the day break   if this day ever break , add new deposit 
 to the crust upon crust of mud , stick at those point 
 tenaciously to the pavement , and accumulate at compound interest . 
 fog everywhere . fog up the river , where it flow among green ait 
 and meadow ; fog down the river , where it roll defile among the 
 tier of shipping and the waterside pollution of a great   and 
 dirty   city . fog on the Essex marsh , fog on the kentish height . 
 fog creep into the caboose of collier - brig ; fog lie out on 
 the yard and hover in the rigging of great ship ; fog drooping 
 on the gunwale of barge and small boat . fog in the eye and 
 throat of ancient Greenwich pensioner , wheeze by the fireside 
 of their ward ; fog in the stem and bowl of the afternoon pipe of 
 the wrathful skipper , down in his close cabin ; fog cruelly pinch 
 the toe and finger of his shiver little ' prentice boy on deck . 
 Chance people on the bridge peep over the parapet into a 
 nether sky of fog , with fog all round they , as if they be up in a 
 balloon and hang in the misty cloud . 
 gas loom through the fog in diver place in the street , much 
 as the sun may , from the spongey field , be see to loom by 
 husbandman and ploughboy . Most of the shop light two hour 
 before their time - as the gas seem to know , for it have a haggard 
 and unwilling look . 
 the raw afternoon be raw , and the dense fog be dense , and the 
 muddy street be muddy near that leaden - head old obstruction , 
 appropriate ornament for the threshold of a leaden - head old 
 corporation , Temple Bar . and hard by Temple Bar , in Lincoln 's Inn 
 Hall , at the very heart of the fog , sit the Lord High Chancellor 
 in his High Court of Chancery . 
 never can there come fog too thick , never can there come mud and 
 mire too deep , to assort with the groping and flounder condition 
 which this High Court of Chancery , most pestilent of hoary sinner , 
 hold this day in the sight of heaven and earth . 
 on such an afternoon , if ever , the Lord High Chancellor ought to be 
 sit here - as here he be - with a foggy glory round his head , 
 softly fence in with crimson cloth and curtain , address by a 
 large advocate with great whisker , a little voice , and an 
 interminable brief , and outwardly direct his contemplation to 
 the lantern in the roof , where he can see nothing but fog . on such 
 an afternoon some score of member of the High Court of Chancery 
 bar ought to be - as here they be - mistily engage in one of the 
 ten thousand stage of an endless cause , trip one another up on 
 slippery precedent , grope knee - deep in technicality , run 
 their goat - hair and horsehair ward head against wall of word 
 and make a pretence of equity with serious face , as player 
 might . on such an afternoon the various solicitor in the cause , 
 some two or three of whom have inherit it from their father , who 
 make a fortune by it , ought to be - as be they not ? - range in a 
 line , in a long matted well   but you might look in vain for truth 
 at the bottom of it   between the registrar 's red table and the silk 
 gown , with bill , cross - bill , answer , rejoinder , injunction , 
 affidavit , issue , reference to master , master ' report , 
 mountain of costly nonsense , pile before they . well may the 
 court be dim , with waste candle here and there ; well may the fog 
 hang heavy in it , as if it would never get out ; well may the 
 stain - glass window lose their colour and admit no light of day 
 into the place ; well may the uninitiate from the street , who peep 
 in through the glass pane in the door , be deter from entrance 
 by its owlish aspect and by the drawl , languidly echo to the 
 roof from the pad dais where the Lord High Chancellor look into 
 the lantern that have no light in it and where the attendant wig 
 be all stick in a fog - bank ! this be the Court of Chancery , which 
 have its decay house and its blighted land in every shire , 
 which have its wear - out lunatic in every madhouse and its dead in 
 every churchyard , which have its ruin suitor with his slipshod 
 heel and threadbare dress borrowing and beg through the round 
 of every man 's acquaintance , which give to monied might the mean 
 abundantly of weary out the right , which so exhaust finance , 
 patience , courage , hope , so overthrow the brain and break the 
 heart , that there be not an honourable man among its practitioner 
 who would not give - who do not often give - the warning , " suffer 
 any wrong that can be do you rather than come here ! " 
 who happen to be in the Lord Chancellor 's court this murky 
 afternoon besides the Lord Chancellor , the counsel in the cause , 
 two or three counsel who be never in any cause , and the well of 
 solicitor before mention ? there be the registrar below the 
 judge , in wig and gown ; and there be two or three mace , or petty- 
 bag , or privy purse , or whatever they may be , in legal court 
 suit . these be all yawn , for no crumb of amusement ever fall 
 from Jarndyce and Jarndyce   the cause in hand , which be squeeze 
 dry year upon year ago . the short - hand writer , the reporter of 
 the court , and the reporter of the newspaper invariably decamp 
 with the rest of the regular when Jarndyce and Jarndyce come on . 
 their place be a blank . stand on a seat at the side of the 
 hall , the well to peer into the curtain sanctuary , be a little 
 mad old woman in a squeeze bonnet who be always in court , from its 
 sit to its rise , and always expect some incomprehensible 
 judgment to be give in her favour . some say she really be , or 
 be , a party to a suit , but no one know for certain because no one 
 care . she carry some small litter in a reticule which she call 
 her document , principally consist of paper match and dry 
 lavender . a sallow prisoner have come up , in custody , for the half- 
 dozenth time to make a personal application " to purge himself of 
 his contempt , " which , be a solitary survive executor who have 
 fall into a state of conglomeration about account of which it be 
 not pretend that he have ever any knowledge , he be not at all 
 likely ever to do . in the meantime his prospect in life be 
 end . another ruin suitor , who periodically appear from 
 Shropshire and break out into effort to address the Chancellor at 
 the close of the day 's business and who can by no mean be make to 
 understand that the Chancellor be legally ignorant of his existence 
 after make it desolate for a quarter of a century , plant himself 
 in a good place and keep an eye on the judge , ready to call out 
 " my Lord ! " in a voice of sonorous complaint on the instant of his 
 rise . a few lawyer ' clerk and other who know this suitor by 
 sight linger on the chance of his furnish some fun and 
 enliven the dismal weather a little . 
 Jarndyce and Jarndyce drone on . this scarecrow of a suit have , in 
 course of time , become so complicated that no man alive know what 
 it mean . the party to it understand it least , but it have be 
 observe that no two Chancery lawyer can talk about it for five 
 minute without come to a total disagreement as to all the 
 premise . innumerable child have be bear into the cause ; 
 innumerable young people have marry into it ; innumerable old 
 people have die out of it . score of person have deliriously 
 find themselves make party in Jarndyce and Jarndyce without 
 know how or why ; whole family have inherit legendary hatred 
 with the suit . the little plaintiff or defendant who be promise 
 a new rock - horse when Jarndyce and Jarndyce should be settle 
 have grow up , possess himself of a real horse , and trot away 
 into the other world . fair ward of court have fade into mother 
 and grandmother ; a long procession of Chancellors have come in and 
 go out ; the legion of bill in the suit have be transform 
 into mere bill of mortality ; there be not three jarndyce leave 
 upon the earth perhaps since old Tom Jarndyce in despair blow his 
 brain out at a coffee - house in Chancery Lane ; but Jarndyce and 
 Jarndyce still drag its dreary length before the court , 
 perennially hopeless . 
 Jarndyce and Jarndyce have pass into a joke . that be the only 
 good that have ever come of it . it have be death to many , but it 
 be a joke in the profession . every master in Chancery have have a 
 reference out of it . every Chancellor be " in it , " for somebody or 
 other , when he be counsel at the bar . good thing have be say 
 about it by blue - nosed , bulbous - shoe old bencher in select port- 
 wine committee after dinner in hall . article clerk have be in 
 the habit of flesh their legal wit upon it . the last Lord 
 Chancellor handle it neatly , when , correct Mr. Blowers , the 
 eminent silk gown who say that such a thing might happen when the 
 sky rain potato , he observe , " or when we get through Jarndyce 
 and Jarndyce , Mr. Blowers " - a pleasantry that particularly tickle 
 the mace , bag , and purse . 
 how many people out of the suit Jarndyce and Jarndyce have stretch 
 forth its unwholesome hand to spoil and corrupt would be a very 
 wide question . from the master upon whose impale file ream of 
 dusty warrant in Jarndyce and Jarndyce have grimly writhe into 
 many shape , down to the copying - clerk in the six Clerks ' Office 
 who have copy his ten of thousand of Chancery folio - page under 
 that eternal heading , no man 's nature have be make well by it . 
 in trickery , evasion , procrastination , spoliation , botheration , 
 under false pretence of all sort , there be influence that can 
 never come to good . the very solicitor ' boy who have keep the 
 wretched suitor at bay , by protest time out of mind that Mr. 
 Chizzle , Mizzle , or otherwise be particularly engage and have 
 appointment until dinner , may have get an extra moral twist and 
 shuffle into themselves out of Jarndyce and Jarndyce . the receiver 
 in the cause have acquire a goodly sum of money by it but have 
 acquire too a distrust of his own mother and a contempt for his 
 own kind . Chizzle , Mizzle , and otherwise have lapse into a habit 
 of vaguely promise themselves that they will look into that 
 outstanding little matter and see what can be do for Drizzle - who 
 be not well use - when Jarndyce and Jarndyce shall be get out of 
 the office . shirk and shark in all their many variety have 
 be sow broadcast by the ill - fated cause ; and even those who have 
 contemplate its history from the outermost circle of such evil 
 have be insensibly tempt into a loose way of let bad thing 
 alone to take their own bad course , and a loose belief that if the 
 world go wrong it be in some off - hand manner never mean to go 
 right . 
 thus , in the midst of the mud and at the heart of the fog , sit the 
 Lord High Chancellor in his High Court of Chancery . 
 " Mr. Tangle , " say the Lord High Chancellor , latterly something 
 restless under the eloquence of that learn gentleman . 
 " Mlud , " say Mr. Tangle . Mr. Tangle know more of Jarndyce and 
 Jarndyce than anybody . he be famous for it - suppose never to have 
 read anything else since he leave school . 
 " have you nearly conclude your argument ? " 
 " Mlud , no - variety of point - feel it my duty tsubmit - ludship , " be 
 the reply that slide out of Mr. Tangle . 
 " several member of the bar be still to be hear , I believe ? " say 
 the Chancellor with a slight smile . 
 eighteen of Mr. Tangle 's learn friend , each arm with a little 
 summary of eighteen hundred sheet , bob up like eighteen hammer in 
 a pianoforte , make eighteen bow , and drop into their eighteen 
 place of obscurity . 
 " we will proceed with the hearing on Wednesday fortnight , " say the 
 Chancellor . for the question at issue be only a question of cost , 
 a mere bud on the forest tree of the parent suit , and really will 
 come to a settlement one of these day . 
 the Chancellor rise ; the bar rise ; the prisoner be bring 
 forward in a hurry ; the man from Shropshire cry , " my lord ! " 
 Maces , bag , and purse indignantly proclaim silence and frown at 
 the man from Shropshire . 
 " in reference , " proceed the Chancellor , still on Jarndyce and 
 Jarndyce , " to the young girl - " 
 " Begludship 's pardon - boy , " say Mr. Tangle prematurely . " in 
 reference , " proceed the Chancellor with extra distinctness , " to 
 the young girl and boy , the two young people " - Mr. Tangle crush - 
 " whom I direct to be in attendance to - day and who be now in my 
 private room , I will see they and satisfy myself as to the 
 expediency of make the order for their reside with their 
 uncle . " 
 Mr. Tangle on his leg again . " Begludship 's pardon - dead . " 
 " with their " - Chancellor look through his double eye - glass at the 
 paper on his desk - " grandfather . " 
 " Begludship 's pardon - victim of rash action - brain . " 
 suddenly a very little counsel with a terrific bass voice arise , 
 fully inflate , in the back settlement of the fog , and say , " will 
 your lordship allow I ? I appear for he . he be a cousin , several 
 time remove . I be not at the moment prepare to inform the court 
 in what exact remove he be a cousin , but he be a cousin . " 
 leave this address   deliver like a sepulchral message   ringing 
 in the rafter of the roof , the very little counsel drop , and the 
 fog know he no more . everybody look for he . nobody can see 
 he . 
 " I will speak with both the young people , " say the Chancellor 
 anew , " and satisfy myself on the subject of their reside with 
 their cousin . I will mention the matter to - morrow morning when I 
 take my seat . " 
 the Chancellor be about to bow to the bar when the prisoner be 
 present . nothing can possibly come of the prisoner 's 
 conglomeration but his be send back to prison , which be soon 
 do . the man from Shropshire venture another remonstrative " my 
 lord ! " but the Chancellor , be aware of he , have dexterously 
 vanish . everybody else quickly vanish too . a battery of blue 
 bag be load with heavy charge of paper and carry off by

 the empty court be lock up . if all the injustice it have 
 commit and all the misery it have cause could only be lock up 
 with it , and the whole burn away in a great funeral pyre - why so 
 much the well for other party than the party in Jarndyce and 
 Jarndyce ! 
 CHAPTER II 
 in fashion 
 it be but a glimpse of the world of fashion that we want on this 
 same miry afternoon . it be not so unlike the Court of Chancery but 
 that we may pass from the one scene to the other , as the crow 
 fly . both the world of fashion and the Court of Chancery be 
 thing of precedent and usage : oversleep Rip Van Winkles who 
 have play at strange game through a deal of thundery weather ; 
 sleep beauty whom the knight will wake one day , when all the 
 stop spit in the kitchen shall begin to turn prodigiously ! 
 it be not a large world . relatively even to this world of our , 
 which have its limit too   as your Highness shall find when you have 
 make the tour of it and be come to the brink of the void beyond , 
 it be a very little speck . there be much good in it ; there be 
 many good and true people in it ; it have its appoint place . but 
 the evil of it be that it be a world wrap up in too much 
 jeweller 's cotton and fine wool , and can not hear the rushing of the 
 large world , and can not see they as they circle round the sun . 
 it be a deaden world , and its growth be sometimes unhealthy for 
 want of air . 
 my Lady Dedlock have return to her house in town for a few day 
 previous to her departure for Paris , where her ladyship intend to 
 stay some week , after which her movement be uncertain . the 
 fashionable intelligence say so for the comfort of the Parisians , 
 and it know all fashionable thing . to know thing otherwise be 
 to be unfashionable . my Lady Dedlock have be down at what she 
 call , in familiar conversation , her " place " in Lincolnshire . the 
 water be out in Lincolnshire . an arch of the bridge in the park 
 have be sap and sop away . the adjacent low - lie ground for 
 half a mile in breadth be a stagnant river with melancholy tree 
 for island in it and a surface puncture all over , all day long , 
 with fall rain . my Lady Dedlock 's place have be extremely 
 dreary . the weather for many a day and night have be so wet that 
 the tree seem wet through , and the soft lopping and pruning of 
 the woodman 's axe can make no crash or crackle as they fall . the 
 deer , look soak , leave quagmire where they pass . the shot of 
 a rifle lose its sharpness in the moist air , and its smoke move 
 in a tardy little cloud towards the green rise , coppice - top , 
 that make a background for the fall rain . the view from my 
 Lady Dedlock 's own window be alternately a lead - colour view and 
 a view in indian ink . the vas on the stone terrace in the 
 foreground catch the rain all day ; and the heavy drop fall - drip , 
 drip , drip - upon the broad flagged pavement , call from old time 
 the Ghost 's Walk , all night . on sunday the little church in the 
 park be mouldy ; the oaken pulpit break out into a cold sweat ; and 
 there be a general smell and taste as of the ancient dedlock in 
 their graf . my Lady Dedlock   who be childless , look out in 
 the early twilight from her boudoir at a keeper 's lodge and see 
 the light of a fire upon the latticed pane , and smoke rise from 
 the chimney , and a child , chase by a woman , run out into the 
 rain to meet the shin figure of a wrap - up man come through 
 the gate , have be put quite out of temper . my Lady Dedlock say 
 she have be " bored to death . " 
 therefore my Lady Dedlock have come away from the place in 
 Lincolnshire and have leave it to the rain , and the crow , and the 
 rabbit , and the deer , and the partridge and pheasant . the 
 picture of the dedlock past and go have seem to vanish into 
 the damp wall in mere lowness of spirit , as the housekeeper have 
 pass along the old room shut up the shutter . and when they 
 will next come forth again , the fashionable intelligence - which , 
 like the fiend , be omniscient of the past and present , but not the 
 future - can not yet undertake to say . 
 Sir Leicester Dedlock be only a baronet , but there be no mighty 
 baronet than he . his family be as old as the hill , and infinitely 
 more respectable . he have a general opinion that the world might 
 get on without hill but would be do up without dedlock . he 
 would on the whole admit nature to be a good idea   a little low , 
 perhaps , when not enclose with a park - fence , but an idea 
 dependent for its execution on your great county family . he be a 
 gentleman of strict conscience , disdainful of all littleness and 
 meanness and ready on the short notice to die any death you may 
 please to mention rather than give occasion for the least 
 impeachment of his integrity . he be an honourable , obstinate , 
 truthful , high - spirit , intensely prejudiced , perfectly 
 unreasonable man . 
 Sir Leicester be twenty year , full measure , old than my Lady . 
 he will never see sixty - five again , nor perhaps sixty - six , nor yet 
 sixty - seven . he have a twist of the gout now and then and walk a 
 little stiffly . he be of a worthy presence , with his light - grey 
 hair and whisker , his fine shirt - frill , his pure - white waistcoat , 
 and his blue coat with bright button always button . he be 
 ceremonious , stately , most polite on every occasion to my Lady , and 
 hold her personal attraction in the high estimation . his 
 gallantry to my Lady , which have never change since he court she , 
 be the one little touch of romantic fancy in he . 
 indeed , he marry she for love . a whisper still go about that 
 she have not even family ; howbeit , Sir Leicester have so much family 
 that perhaps he have enough and could dispense with any more . but 
 she have beauty , pride , ambition , insolent resolve , and sense enough 
 to portion out a legion of fine lady . wealth and station , add 
 to these , soon float she upward , and for year now my Lady 
 Dedlock have be at the centre of the fashionable intelligence and 
 at the top of the fashionable tree . 
 how Alexander weep when he have no more world to conquer , everybody 
 know - or have some reason to know by this time , the matter have 
 be rather frequently mention . my Lady Dedlock , have 
 conquer her world , fall not into the melting , but rather into the 
 freezing , mood . an exhausted composure , a wear - out placidity , an 
 equanimity of fatigue not to be ruffle by interest or satisfaction , 
 be the trophy of her victory . she be perfectly well - breed . 
 if she could be translate to heaven to - morrow , she might be 
 expect to ascend without any rapture . 
 she have beauty still , and if it be not in its heyday , it be not yet 
 in its autumn . she have a fine face - originally of a character that 
 would be rather call very pretty than handsome , but improve into 
 classicality by the acquire expression of her fashionable state . 
 her figure be elegant and have the effect of be tall . not that 
 she be so , but that " the most be make , " as the Honourable Bob 
 Stables have frequently assert upon oath , " of all her point . " 
 the same authority observe that she be perfectly get up and 
 remark in commendation of her hair especially that she be the 
 well - groom woman in the whole stud . 
 with all her perfection on her head , my Lady Dedlock have come up 
 from her place in Lincolnshire   hotly pursue by the fashionable 
 intelligence   to pass a few day at her house in town previous to 
 her departure for Paris , where her ladyship intend to stay some 
 week , after which her movement be uncertain . and at her house 
 in town , upon this muddy , murky afternoon , present himself an old- 
 fashioned old gentleman , attorney - at - law and eke solicitor of the 
 High Court of Chancery , who have the honour of act as legal 
 adviser of the Dedlocks and have as many cast - iron box in his 
 office with that name outside as if the present baronet be the 
 coin of the conjuror 's trick and be constantly be juggle 
 through the whole set . across the hall , and up the stair , and 
 along the passage , and through the room , which be very brilliant 
 in the season and very dismal out of it - fairy - land to visit , but a 
 desert to live in - the old gentleman be conduct by a Mercury in 
 powder to my Lady 's presence . 
 the old gentleman be rusty to look at , but be repute to have make 
 good thrift out of aristocratic marriage settlement and 
 aristocratic will , and to be very rich . he be surround by a 
 mysterious halo of family confidence , of which he be know to be 
 the silent depository . there be noble mausoleum root for 
 century in retire glade of park among the grow timber and 
 the fern , which perhaps hold few noble secret than walk abroad 
 among man , shut up in the breast of Mr. Tulkinghorn . he be of what 
 be call the old school - a phrase generally mean any school 
 that seem never to have be young - and wear knee - breech tie 
 with ribbon , and gaiter or stocking . one peculiarity of his 
 black clothe and of his black stocking , be they silk or worst , 
 be that they never shine . mute , close , irresponsive to any 
 glancing light , his dress be like himself . he never converse when 
 not professionaly consult . he be find sometimes , speechless but 
 quite at home , at corner of dinner - table in great country house 
 and near door of drawing - room , concern which the fashionable 
 intelligence be eloquent , where everybody know he and where half 
 the Peerage stop to say " how do you do , Mr. Tulkinghorn ? " he 
 receive these salutation with gravity and bury they along with 
 the rest of his knowledge . 
 Sir Leicester Dedlock be with my Lady and be happy to see Mr. 
 Tulkinghorn . there be an air of prescription about he which be 
 always agreeable to Sir Leicester ; he receive it as a kind of 
 tribute . he like Mr. Tulkinghorn 's dress ; there be a kind of 
 tribute in that too . it be eminently respectable , and likewise , in 
 a general way , retainer - like . it express , as it be , the 
 steward of the legal mystery , the butler of the legal cellar , of 
 the Dedlocks . 
 have Mr. Tulkinghorn any idea of this himself ? it may be so , or it 
 may not , but there be this remarkable circumstance to be note in 
 everything associate with my Lady Dedlock as one of a class - as 
 one of the leader and representative of her little world . she 
 suppose herself to be an inscrutable being , quite out of the reach 
 and ken of ordinary mortal - see herself in her glass , where 
 indeed she look so . yet every dim little star revolve about 
 she , from she maid to the manager of the Italian Opera , know she 
 weakness , prejudice , folly , haughtiness , and caprice and 
 live upon as accurate a calculation and as nice a measure of her 
 moral nature as her dressmaker take of her physical proportion . 
 be a new dress , a new custom , a new singer , a new dancer , a new 
 form of jewellery , a new dwarf or giant , a new chapel , a new 
 anything , to be set up ? there be deferential people in a dozen 
 calling whom my Lady Dedlock suspect of nothing but prostration 
 before she , who can tell you how to manage she as if she be a 
 baby , who do nothing but nurse she all their life , who , humbly 
 affect to follow with profound subservience , lead she and her 
 whole troop after they ; who , in hook one , hook all and bear they 
 off as Lemuel Gulliver bear away the stately fleet of the majestic 
 Lilliput . " if you want to address our people , sir , " say Blaze and 
 Sparkle , the jeweller - meaning by our people Lady Dedlock and the 
 rest - " you must remember that you be not deal with the general 
 public ; you must hit our people in their weak place , and their 
 weak place be such a place . " " to make this article go down , 
 gentleman , " say Sheen and Gloss , the mercer , to their friend the 
 manufacturer , " you must come to we , because we know where to have 
 the fashionable people , and we can make it fashionable . " " if you 
 want to get this print upon the table of my high connexion , sir , " 
 say Mr. Sladdery , the librarian , " or if you want to get this dwarf 
 or giant into the house of my high connexion , sir , or if you want 
 to secure to this entertainment the patronage of my high connexion , 
 sir , you must leave it , if you please , to I , for I have be 
 accustom to study the leader of my high connexion , sir , and I 
 may tell you without vanity that I can turn they round my finger " - 
 in which Mr. Sladdery , who be an honest man , do not exaggerate at 
 all . 
 therefore , while Mr. Tulkinghorn may not know what be pass in 
 the Dedlock mind at present , it be very possible that he may . 
 " my Lady 's cause have be again before the Chancellor , have it , Mr. 
 Tulkinghorn ? " say Sir Leicester , give he his hand .

 make one of his quiet bow to my Lady , who be on a sofa near the 
 fire , shade her face with a hand - screen . 
 " it would be useless to ask , " say my Lady with the dreariness of 
 the place in Lincolnshire still upon she , " whether anything have 
 be do . " 
 " nothing that you would call anything have be do to - day , " 
 reply Mr. Tulkinghorn . 
 " nor ever will be , " say my Lady . 
 Sir Leicester have no objection to an interminable Chancery suit . 
 it be a slow , expensive , british , constitutional kind of thing . to 
 be sure , he have not a vital interest in the suit in question , her 
 part in which be the only property my Lady bring he ; and he have

 in a cause , and not in the title of that cause , be a most 
 ridiculous accident . but he regard the Court of Chancery , even if 
 it should involve an occasional delay of justice and a trifling

 variety of other something by the perfection of human wisdom for 
 the eternal settlement   humanly speak   of everything . and he be 
 upon the whole of a fix opinion that to give the sanction of his 
 countenance to any complaint respect it would be to encourage 
 some person in the low class to rise up somewhere - like Wat 
 Tyler . 
 " as a few fresh affidavit have be put upon the file , " say Mr. 
 Tulkinghorn , " and as they be short , and as I proceed upon the 
 troublesome principle of beg leave to possess my client with 
 any new proceeding in a cause " - cautious man Mr. Tulkinghorn , 
 take no more responsibility than necessary - " and far , as I 
 see you be go to Paris , I have bring they in my pocket . " 
  Sir Leicester be go to Paris too , by the by , but the delight 
 of the fashionable intelligence be in his Lady . 
 Mr. Tulkinghorn take out his paper , ask permission to place they 
 on a golden talisman of a table at my Lady 's elbow , put on his

 " ' in Chancery . between John Jarndyce - ' " 
 my Lady interrupt , request he to miss as many of the formal 
 horror as he can .

 hand be form . why do you ask ? " 
 " anything to vary this detestable monotony . oh , go on , do ! "

 her face . Sir Leicester doze , start up suddenly , and cry , " Eh ? 
 what do you say ? "

 " Faint , " my Lady murmur with white lip , " only that ; but it be 
 like the faintness of death . do not   speak to I . Ring , and take I

 Mr. Tulkinghorn retire into another chamber ; bell ring , foot

 CHAPTER III 
 A Progress 
 I have a great deal of difficulty in begin to write my portion 
 of these page , for I know I be not clever . I always know that . I 
 can remember , when I be a very little girl indeed , I use to say 
 to my doll when we be alone together , " now , dolly , I be not 
 clever , you know very well , and you must be patient with I , like a 
 dear ! " and so she use to sit prop up in a great arm - chair , 
 with her beautiful complexion and rosy lip , star at I - or not 
 so much at I , I think , as at nothing - while I busily stitch away 
 and tell she every one of my secret . 
 my dear old doll ! I be such a shy little thing that I seldom 
 dare to open my lip , and never dare to open my heart , to anybody 
 else . it almost make I cry to think what a relief it use to be 
 to I when I come home from school of a day to run upstairs to my 
 room and say , " oh , you dear faithful Dolly , I know you would be 
 expect I ! " and then to sit down on the floor , lean on the 
 elbow of her great chair , and tell she all I have notice since we 
 part . I have always rather a noticing way - not a quick way , oh , 
 no ! - a silent way of notice what pass before I and think I 
 should like to understand it well . I have not by any mean a 
 quick understanding . when I love a person very tenderly indeed , it 
 seem to brighten . but even that may be my vanity . 
 I be bring up , from my early remembrance - like some of the 
 princess in the fairy story , only I be not charming - by my 
 godmother . at least , I only know she as such . she be a good , 
 good woman ! she go to church three time every Sunday , and to 
 morning prayer on wednesday and Fridays , and to lecture whenever 
 there be lecture ; and never miss . she be handsome ; and if 
 she have ever smile , would have be   I use to think   like an 
 angel - but she never smile . she be always grave and strict . she 
 be so very good herself , I think , that the badness of other 
 people make she frown all her life . I feel so different from she , 
 even make every allowance for the difference between a child and 
 a woman ; I feel so poor , so trifling , and so far off that I never 
 could be unrestrained with she - no , could never even love she as I 
 wish . it make I very sorry to consider how good she be and how 
 unworthy of she I be , and I use ardently to hope that I might 
 have a well heart ; and I talk it over very often with the dear 
 old doll , but I never love my godmother as I ought to have love 
 she and as I feel I must have love she if I have be a well 
 girl . 
 this make I , I dare say , more timid and retire than I naturally 
 be and cast I upon dolly as the only friend with whom I feel at 
 ease . but something happen when I be still quite a little thing 
 that help it very much . 
 I have never hear my mama speak of . I have never hear of my papa 
 either , but I feel more interested about my mama . I have never wear

 mama 's grave . I have never be tell where it be . yet I have never

 our only servant , who take my light away when I be in bed   another 
 very good woman , but austere to I , and she have only say ,

 although there be seven girl at the neighbouring school where I 
 be a day boarder , and although they call I little Esther

 than I , to be sure   I be the young there by a good deal , but 
 there seem to be some other separation between we besides that , 
 and besides their be far more clever than I be and know much 
 more than I do . one of they in the first week of my go to the 
 school   I remember it very well   invite I home to a little party , 
 to my great joy . but my godmother write a stiff letter decline 
 for I , and I never go . I never go out at all . 
 it be my birthday . there be holiday at school on other 
 birthday - none on mine . there be rejoicing at home on other 
 birthday , as I know from what I hear the girl relate to one 
 another - there be none on mine . my birthday be the most 
 melancholy day at home in the whole year . 
 I have mention that unless my vanity should deceive I   as I know 
 it may , for I may be very vain without suspect it , though indeed 
 I do not   , my comprehension be quicken when my affection be . my 
 disposition be very affectionate , and perhaps I might still feel 
 such a wound if such a wound could be receive more than once with 
 the quickness of that birthday . 
 Dinner be over , and my godmother and I be sit at the table 
 before the fire . the clock tick , the fire click ; not another 
 sound have be hear in the room or in the house for I do not   know 
 how long . I happen to look timidly up from my stitching , across 
 the table at my godmother , and I see in her face , look gloomily 
 at I , " it would have be far well , little Esther , that you have 
 have no birthday , that you have never be bear ! " 
 I break out cry and sobbing , and I say , " oh , dear godmother , 
 tell I , pray do tell I , do Mama die on my birthday ? " 
 " no , " she return . " ask I no more , child ! " 
 " oh , do pray tell I something of she . do now , at last , dear 
 godmother , if you please ! what do I do to she ? how do I lose 
 she ? why be I so different from other child , and why be it my 
 fault , dear godmother ? no , no , no , do not   go away . oh , speak to 
 I ! " 
 I be in a kind of fright beyond my grief , and I catch hold of her 
 dress and be kneel to she . she have be say all the while , 
 " let I go ! " but now she stand still . 
 her darken face have such power over I that it stop I in the 
 midst of my vehemence . I put up my trembling little hand to clasp 
 her or to beg her pardon with what earnestness I might , but 
 withdraw it as she look at I , and lay it on my flutter 
 heart . she raise I , sit in her chair , and stand I before

 be her . the time will come - and soon enough - when you will

 she check I , however , as I be about to depart from she - so

 dear , dear , to think how much time we pass alone together

 there ! I have wipe they away now and can go on again properly .

 one sunny afternoon when I have come home from school with my book

 it must have be two year afterwards , and I be almost fourteen ,

 he appear to enjoy beyond everything the sound of his own voice .

 this interview take place at Windsor , where I have pass   as far as

 CHAPTER IV 
 Telescopic Philanthropy

 A Morning Adventure

 quite at home

 people - "

 brighten more and more .

 we all confirm it from our night 's experience .

 Ada and I agree , as we talk together for a little while upstairs , 
 that this caprice about the wind be a fiction and that he use the

 CHAPTER VII 
 the Ghost 's Walk 
 while Esther sleep , and while Esther wake , it be still wet weather 
 down at the place in Lincolnshire . the rain be ever fall - drip , 
 drip , drip - by day and night upon the broad flagged terrace- 
 pavement , the Ghost 's Walk . the weather be so very bad down in 
 Lincolnshire that the lively imagination can scarcely apprehend 
 its ever be fine again . not that there be any superabundant life 
 of imagination on the spot , for Sir Leicester be not here   and , 
 truly , even if he be , would not do much for it in that 
 particular , but be in Paris with my Lady ; and solitude , with dusky 
 wing , sit brood upon Chesney Wold . 
 there may be some motion of fancy among the low animal at 
 Chesney Wold . the horse in the stable - the long stable in a 
 barren , red - brick court - yard , where there be a great bell in a 
 turret , and a clock with a large face , which the pigeon who live 
 near it and who love to perch upon its shoulder seem to be always 
 consult - they may contemplate some mental picture of fine 
 weather on occasion , and may be well artist at they than the 
 groom . the old roan , so famous for cross - country work , turn his 
 large eyeball to the grated window near his rack , may remember the 
 fresh leaf that glisten there at other time and the scent that 
 stream in , and may have a fine run with the hound , while the human 
 helper , clear out the next stall , never stir beyond his 
 pitchfork and birch - broom . the grey , whose place be opposite the 
 door and who with an impatient rattle of his halter prick his ear 
 and turn his head so wistfully when it be open , and to whom the 
 opener say , " woa grey , then , steady ! noabody want you to - day ! " 
 may know it quite as well as the man . the whole seemingly 
 monotonous and uncompanionable half - dozen , stable together , may 
 pass the long wet hour when the door be shut in lively 
 communication than be hold in the servant ' hall or at the Dedlock 
 arm , or may even beguile the time by improve   perhaps corrupt 
 the pony in the loose - box in the corner . 
 so the mastiff , doze in his kennel in the court - yard with his 
 large head on his paw , may think of the hot sunshine when the 
 shadow of the stable - building tire his patience out by change 
 and leave he at one time of the day no broad refuge than the 
 shadow of his own house , where he sit on end , pant and growling 
 short , and very much want something to worry besides himself and 
 his chain . so now , half - waking and all - wink , he may recall the 
 house full of company , the coach - house full of vehicle , the 
 stable full of horse , and the out - building full of attendant 
 upon horse , until he be undecided about the present and come forth 
 to see how it be . then , with that impatient shake of himself , he 
 may growl in the spirit , " rain , rain , rain ! nothing but rain - and 
 no family here ! " as he go in again and lie down with a gloomy 
 yawn . 
 so with the dog in the kennel - building across the park , who have 
 their restless fit and whose doleful voice when the wind have be 
 very obstinate have even make it know in the house itself - 
 upstairs , downstair , and in my Lady 's chamber . they may hunt the 
 whole country - side , while the raindrop be patter round their 
 inactivity . so the rabbit with their self - betray tail , 
 frisk in and out of hole at root of tree , may be lively with 
 idea of the breezy day when their ear be blow about or of those 
 season of interest when there be sweet young plant to gnaw . the 
 turkey in the poultry - yard , always trouble with a class - grievance 
  probably Christmas , may be reminiscent of that summer morning 
 wrongfully take from he when he get into the lane among the felled 
 tree , where there be a barn and barley . the discontent goose , 
 who stoop to pass under the old gateway , twenty foot high , may 
 gabble out , if we only know it , a waddle preference for weather 
 when the gateway cast its shadow on the ground . 
 be this as it may , there be not much fancy otherwise stir at 
 Chesney Wold . if there be a little at any odd moment , it go , 
 like a little noise in that old echo place , a long way and 
 usually lead off to ghost and mystery . 
 it have rain so hard and rain so long down in Lincolnshire that 
 Mrs. Rouncewell , the old housekeeper at Chesney Wold , have several 
 time take off her spectacle and clean they to make certain 
 that the drop be not upon the glass . Mrs. Rouncewell might 
 have be sufficiently assure by hear the rain , but that she be 
 rather deaf , which nothing will induce she to believe . she be a 
 fine old lady , handsome , stately , wonderfully neat , and have such a 
 back and such a stomacher that if her stay should turn out when 
 she die to have be a broad old - fashioned family fire - grate , 
 nobody who know she would have cause to be surprised . weather 
 affect Mrs. Rouncewell little . the house be there in all 
 weather , and the house , as she express it , " be what she look 
 at . " she sit in her room   in a side passage on the ground floor , 
 with an arched window command a smooth quadrangle , adorn at 
 regular interval with smooth round tree and smooth round block 
 of stone , as if the tree be go to play at bowl with the 
 stone , and the whole house repose on her mind . she can open it 
 on occasion and be busy and flutter , but it be shut up now and 
 lie on the breadth of Mrs. Rouncewell 's iron - bind bosom in a

 it be the next difficult thing to an impossibility to imagine

 fifty year . ask she how long , this rainy day , and she shall 
 answer " fifty year , three month , and a fortnight , by the blessing 
 of heaven , if I live till Tuesday . " Mr. Rouncewell die some time 
 before the decease of the pretty fashion of pig - tail , and modestly 
 hide his own   if he take it with he   in a corner of the churchyard

 the present representative of the Dedlocks be an excellent master .

 be well ? "

 CHAPTER VIII 
 cover a Multitude of Sins 
 it be interesting when I dress before daylight to peep out of 
 window , where my candle be reflect in the black pane like two 
 beacon , and find all beyond still enshroud in the 
 indistinctness of last night , to watch how it turn out when the 
 day come on . as the prospect gradually reveal itself and 
 disclose the scene over which the wind have wander in the dark , 
 like my memory over my life , I have a pleasure in discover the 
 unknown object that have be around I in my sleep . at first they 
 be faintly discernible in the mist , and above they the later 
 star still glimmer . that pale interval over , the picture begin 
 to enlarge and fill up so fast that at every new peep I could have 
 find enough to look at for an hour . imperceptibly my candle 
 become the only incongruous part of the morning , the dark place in 
 my room all melt away , and the day shine bright upon a cheerful 
 landscape , prominent in which the old Abbey Church , with its 
 massive tower , throw a soft train of shadow on the view than 
 seem compatible with its rugged character . but so from rough 
 outside   I hope I have learn , serene and gentle influence often 
 proceed .

 CHAPTER ix 
 Signs and Tokens 
 I do not   know how it be I seem to be always write about myself . I 
 mean all the time to write about other people , and I try to think 
 about myself as little as possible , and I be sure , when I find 
 myself come into the story again , I be really vex and say , 
 " dear , dear , you tiresome little creature , I wish you would not ! " 
 but it be all of no use . I hope any one who may read what I write 
 will understand that if these page contain a great deal about I , 
 I can only suppose it must be because I have really something to do 
 with they and can not   be keep out . 
 my darling and I read together , and work , and practise , and 
 find so much employment for our time that the winter day fly by 
 we like bright - wing bird . generally in the afternoon , and 
 always in the evening , Richard give we his company . although he 
 be one of the most restless creature in the world , he certainly 
 be very fond of our society . 
 he be very , very , very fond of Ada . I mean it , and I have well 
 say it at once . I have never see any young people fall in love 
 before , but I find they out quite soon . I could not say so , of 
 course , or show that I know anything about it . on the contrary , I 
 be so demure and use to seem so unconscious that sometimes I 
 consider within myself while I be sit at work whether I be 
 not grow quite deceitful . 
 but there be no help for it . all I have to do be to be quiet , and 
 I be as quiet as a mouse . they be as quiet as mouse too , so far 
 as any word be concern , but the innocent manner in which they 
 rely more and more upon I as they take more and more to one 
 another be so charming that I have great difficulty in not show 
 how it interest I . 
 " our dear little old woman be such a capital old woman , " Richard 
 would say , come up to meet I in the garden early , with his 
 pleasant laugh and perhaps the least tinge of a blush , " that I 
 can not   get on without she . before I begin my harum - scarum day - 
 grind away at those book and instrument and then gallop up 
 hill and down dale , all the country round , like a highwayman - it 
 do I so much good to come and have a steady walk with our 
 comfortable friend , that here I be again ! " 
 " you know , Dame Durden , dear , " Ada would say at night , with she 
 head upon my shoulder and the firelight shin in her thoughtful 
 eye , " I do not   want to talk when we come upstairs here . only to 
 sit a little while think , with your dear face for company , and 
 to hear the wind and remember the poor sailor at sea - " 
 ah ! perhaps Richard be go to be a sailor . we have talk it 
 over very often now , and there be some talk of gratify the

 send her compliment to the young gentleman   to whom she perfectly 
 remember that she be ally by remote consanguinity   and trust

 " so I apprehend it be pretty clear , " say Richard to I , " that I

 with a buoyancy and hopefulness and a gaiety that hardly ever

 " how be that ? " say I. 
 " why , I get rid of ten pound which I be quite content to get rid

 " no , " say I. 
 " very well ! then I come into possession of ten pound - " 
 " the same ten pound , " I hint .

 ten pound more than I expect to have , and consequently I can

 in exactly the same way , when he be persuade out of the sacrifice

 I believe Richard 's be as frank and generous a nature as there

 talk thus , they go upstairs , and presently we hear he in his

 we all conceive a prepossession in his favour , for there be a

 the subject of this laudation be a very little canary , who be so

 the whole bar . " 
 " there never be such an infernal cauldron as that Chancery on the

 it be impossible not to laugh at the energetic gravity with which

 country seem to echo to his " ha , ha , ha ! " it have not the least

 " but how do you and your neighbour get on about the dispute right

 " complimentary to our distant relation ! " say my guardian

 CHAPTER X 
 the Law - Writer 
 on the eastern border of Chancery Lane , that be to say , more 
 particularly in Cook 's Court , Cursitor Street , Mr. Snagsby , law- 
 stationer , pursue his lawful calling . in the shade of Cook 's 
 Court , at most time a shady place , Mr. Snagsby have deal in all 
 sort of blank form of legal process ; in skin and roll of 
 parchment ; in paper - foolscap , brief , draft , brown , white , whitey- 
 brown , and blot ; in stamp ; in office - quill , pen , ink , india- 
 rubber , pounce , pin , pencil , sealing - wax , and wafer ; in red tape 
 and green ferret ; in pocket - book , almanac , diary , and law list ; 
 in string box , ruler , inkstand - glass and leaden - pen - knife , 
 scissor , bodkin , and other small office - cutlery ; in short , in 
 article too numerous to mention , ever since he be out of his time 
 and go into partnership with Peffer . on that occasion , Cook 's 
 Court be in a manner revolutionize by the new inscription in fresh 
 paint , PEFFER and SNAGSBY , displace the time - honour and not 
 easily to be decipher legend PEFFER only . for smoke , which be the 
 London ivy , have so wreathe itself round Peffer 's name and clung to 
 his dwelling - place that the affectionate parasite quite overpowered 
 the parent tree . 
 Peffer be never see in Cook 's Court now . he be not expect there , 
 for he have be recumbent this quarter of a century in the 
 churchyard of St. Andrews , Holborn , with the waggon and hackney- 
 coach roar past he all the day and half the night like one 
 great dragon . if he ever steal forth when the dragon be at rest to 
 air himself again in Cook 's Court until admonish to return by the 
 crowing of the sanguine cock in the cellar at the little dairy in 
 Cursitor Street , whose idea of daylight it would be curious to 
 ascertain , since he know from his personal observation next to 
 nothing about it - if Peffer ever do revisit the pale glimpse of 
 Cook 's Court , which no law - stationer in the trade can positively 
 deny , he come invisibly , and no one be the bad or wiser . 
 in his lifetime , and likewise in the period of Snagsby 's " time " of 
 seven long year , there dwelt with Peffer in the same law- 
 stationering premise a niece - a short , shrewd niece , something too 
 violently compress about the waist , and with a sharp nose like a 
 sharp autumn evening , incline to be frosty towards the end . the 
 Cook 's Courtiers have a rumour fly among they that the mother of 
 this niece do , in her daughter 's childhood , move by too jealous a 
 solicitude that her figure should approach perfection , lace she up

 strong hold and purchase ; and far , that she exhibit 
 internally pint of vinegar and lemon - juice , which acid , they hold , 
 have mount to the nose and temper of the patient . with whichsoever

 estate , enter into two partnership at once . so now , in Cook 's

 niece still cherish her figure , which , however taste may differ ,

 Mr. and Mrs. Snagsby be not only one bone and one flesh , but , to

 proceed from Mrs. Snagsby alone , be hear in Cook 's Court very 
 often . Mr. Snagsby , otherwise than as he find expression through 
 these dulcet tone , be rarely hear . he be a mild , bald , timid man 
 with a shin head and a scrubby clump of black hair stick out

 woman be a - give it to Guster ! " 
 this proper name , so use by Mr. Snagsby , have before now sharpen

 name of Mrs. Snagsby , see that she might with great force and

 fill with clothing , of a lean young woman from a workhouse   by

 farm or contract for during her grow time by an amiable

 the day be close in and the gas be light , but be not yet fully

 here , in a large house , formerly a house of state , live Mr. 
 Tulkinghorn . it be let off in set of chamber now , and in those

 like as he be to look at , so be his apartment in the dusk of the

 here , beneath the paint ceiling , with foreshortened Allegory

 our Dear Brother

 CHAPTER XII 
 on the Watch 
 it have leave off rain down in Lincolnshire at last , and Chesney 
 Wold have take heart . Mrs. Rouncewell be full of hospitable care , 
 for Sir Leicester and my Lady be come home from Paris . the 
 fashionable intelligence have find it out and communicate the glad 
 tiding to benighted England . it have also find out that they will 
 entertain a brilliant and distinguished circle of the elite of the 
 BEAU MONDE   the fashionable intelligence be weak in English , but a 
 giant refresh in French   at the ancient and hospitable family seat 
 in Lincolnshire . 
 for the great honour of the brilliant and distinguished circle , 
 and of Chesney Wold into the bargain , the break arch of the bridge 
 in the park be mend ; and the water , now retire within its proper 
 limit and again span gracefully , make a figure in the prospect 
 from the house . the clear , cold sunshine glance into the brittle 
 wood and approvingly behold the sharp wind scatter the leaf 
 and dry the moss . it glide over the park after the move 
 shadow of the cloud , and chase they , and never catch they , all 
 day . it look in at the window and touch the ancestral portrait 
 with bar and patch of brightness never contemplate by the 
 painter . athwart the picture of my Lady , over the great chimney- 
 piece , it throw a broad bend - sinister of light that strike down 
 crookedly into the hearth and seem to rend it . 
 through the same cold sunshine and the same sharp wind , my Lady and 
 Sir Leicester , in their travel chariot   my Lady 's woman and Sir 
 Leicester 's man affectionate in the rumble , start for home . with a 
 considerable amount of jingling and whip - crack , and many plunge 
 demonstration on the part of two bare - back horse and two 
 centaur with glaze hat , jack - boot , and flow mane and tail , 
 they rattle out of the yard of the Hotel Bristol in the Place 
 vendome and canter between the sun - and - shadow - chequer colonnade of 
 the Rue de Rivoli and the garden of the ill - fat palace of a 
 headless king and queen , off by the Place of Concord , and the 
 Elysian Fields , and the Gate of the Star , out of Paris . 
 Sooth to say , they can not go away too fast , for even here my Lady 
 Dedlock have be bore to death . Concert , assembly , opera , theatre , 
 drive , nothing be new to my Lady under the wear - out heaven . only 
 last Sunday , when poor wretch be gay - within the wall play 
 with child among the clip tree and the statue in the Palace 
 Garden ; walking , a score abreast , in the Elysian Fields , make more 
 Elysian by perform dog and wooden horse ; between while 
 filtering   a few   through the gloomy Cathedral of our Lady to say a 
 word or two at the base of a pillar within flare of a rusty little 
 gridiron - full of gusty little taper ; without the wall encompass 
 Paris with dancing , love - making , wine - drinking , tobacco - smoking , 
 tomb - visit , billiard card and domino playing , quack - doctoring , 
 and much murderous refuse , animate and inanimate - only last Sunday , 
 my Lady , in the desolation of Boredom and the clutch of Giant 
 despair , almost hat her own maid for be in spirit . 
 she can not , therefore , go too fast from Paris . weariness of soul 
 lie before she , as it lie behind - her Ariel have put a girdle of it 
 round the whole earth , and it can not be unclasp - but the imperfect 
 remedy be always to fly from the last place where it have be 
 experienced . Fling Paris back into the distance , then , exchange 
 it for endless avenue and cross - avenue of wintry tree ! and , when 
 next beheld , let it be some league away , with the Gate of the Star 
 a white speck glitter in the sun , and the city a mere mound in a 
 plain - two dark square tower rise out of it , and light and shadow 
 descend on it aslant , like the angel in Jacob 's dream ! 
 Sir Leicester be generally in a complacent state , and rarely bored . 
 when he have nothing else to do , he can always contemplate his own 
 greatness . it be a considerable advantage to a man to have so 
 inexhaustible a subject . after read his letter , he lean back 
 in his corner of the carriage and generally review his importance 
 to society . 
 " you have an unusual amount of correspondence this morning ? " say my 
 Lady after a long time . she be fatigue with reading . have almost 
 read a page in twenty mile . 
 " nothing in it , though . nothing whatever . "

 the sea have no appreciation of great man , but knock they about like

 " ' tis almost a pity , " Mrs. Rouncewell add - only " almost " because it

 Lady have no family . if she have have a daughter now , a grown young

 " might not that have make she still more proud , grandmother ? " say 
 Watt , who have be home and come back again , he be such a good

 " I beg your pardon , grandmother . but she be proud , be she not ? "

 " surely , none in the world , child . " 
 " I be glad of that , " say Watt , " because I have an inexpressible

 he happen to glance at Rosa , who look down and be very shy indeed .

 my Lady 's maid be a Frenchwoman of two and thirty , from somewhere in

 ha , ha , ha ! she , Hortense , be in my Lady 's service since five

 Esther 's Narrative

 Deportment

 CHAPTER XVI 
 Tom - all - Alone 's 
 my Lady Dedlock be restless , very restless . the astonished 
 fashionable intelligence hardly know where to have she . to - day 
 she be at Chesney Wold ; yesterday she be at her house in town ; to- 
 morrow she may be abroad , for anything the fashionable intelligence 
 can with confidence predict . even Sir Leicester 's gallantry have 
 some trouble to keep pace with she . it would have more but that 
 his other faithful ally , for well and for bad - the gout - dart 
 into the old oak bed - chamber at Chesney Wold and grip he by both 
 leg . 
 Sir Leicester receive the gout as a troublesome demon , but still a 
 demon of the patrician order . all the dedlock , in the direct male 
 line , through a course of time during and beyond which the memory 
 of man goeth not to the contrary , have have the gout . it can be 
 prove , sir . other man 's father may have die of the rheumatism 
 or may have take base contagion from the tainted blood of the sick 
 vulgar , but the Dedlock family have communicate something 
 exclusive even to the levelling process of die by die of their 
 own family gout . it have come down through the illustrious line 
 like the plate , or the picture , or the place in Lincolnshire . it 
 be among their dignity . Sir Leicester be perhaps not wholly 
 without an impression , though he have never resolve it into word , 
 that the angel of death in the discharge of his necessary duty 
 may observe to the shade of the aristocracy , " my lord and 
 gentleman , I have the honour to present to you another Dedlock 
 certify to have arrive per the family gout . " 
 hence Sir Leicester yield up his family leg to the family 
 disorder as if he hold his name and fortune on that feudal tenure . 
 he feel that for a Dedlock to be lay upon his back and 
 spasmodically twitch and stab in his extremity be a liberty 
 take somewhere , but he think , " we have all yield to this ; it 
 belong to we ; it have for some hundred of year be understand 
 that we be not to make the vault in the park interesting on more 
 ignoble term ; and I submit myself to the compromise . " 
 and a goodly show he make , lie in a flush of crimson and gold in 
 the midst of the great drawing - room before his favourite picture of 
 my Lady , with broad strip of sunlight shin in , down the long 
 perspective , through the long line of window , and alternate with 
 soft relief of shadow . outside , the stately oak , root for age 
 in the green ground which have never know ploughshare , but be 
 still a chase when king ride to battle with sword and shield and 
 ride a - hunting with bow and arrow , bear witness to his greatness . 
 inside , his forefather , look on he from the wall , say , " each 
 of we be a pass reality here and leave this coloured shadow of 
 himself and melt into remembrance as dreamy as the distant voice 
 of the rook now lull you to rest , " and hear their testimony to 
 his greatness too . and he be very great this day . and woe to 
 Boythorn or other daring wight who shall presumptuously contest an 
 inch with he ! 
 my Lady be at present represent , near Sir Leicester , by her 
 portrait . she have flit away to town , with no intention of 
 remain there , and will soon flit hither again , to the confusion 
 of the fashionable intelligence . the house in town be not prepare 
 for her reception . it be muffled and dreary . only one Mercury in 
 powder gape disconsolate at the hall - window ; and he mention last 
 night to another Mercury of his acquaintance , also accustom to 
 good society , that if that sort of thing be to last - which it 
 could not , for a man of his spirit could not   bear it , and a man of 
 his figure could not   be expect to bear it - there would be no 
 resource for he , upon his honour , but to cut his throat ! 
 what connexion can there be between the place in Lincolnshire , the 
 house in town , the Mercury in powder , and the whereabout of Jo the 
 outlaw with the broom , who have that distant ray of light upon he 
 when he sweep the churchyard - step ? what connexion can there have 
 be between many people in the innumerable history of this world 
 who from opposite side of great gulfs have , nevertheless , be 
 very curiously bring together ! 
 Jo sweep his crossing all day long , unconscious of the link , if 
 any link there be . he sum up his mental condition when ask a 
 question by reply that he " do not   know nothink . " he know that 
 it be hard to keep the mud off the crossing in dirty weather , and 
 hard still to live by do it . nobody teach he even that 
 much ; he find it out . 
 Jo live - that be to say , Jo have not yet die - in a ruinous place 
 know to the like of he by the name of Tom - all - Alone 's . it be a 
 black , dilapidated street , avoid by all decent people , where the 
 crazy house be seize upon , when their decay be far advanced , 
 by some bold vagrant who after establish their own possession 
 take to let they out in lodging . now , these tumble 
 tenement contain , by night , a swarm of misery . as on the ruin 
 human wretch vermin parasite appear , so these ruin shelter have 
 breed a crowd of foul existence that crawl in and out of gap in 
 wall and board ; and coil itself to sleep , in maggot number , 
 where the rain drip in ; and come and go , fetch and carry 
 fever and sow more evil in its every footprint than Lord Coodle , 
 and Sir Thomas Doodle , and the Duke of Foodle , and all the fine 
 gentleman in office , down to Zoodle , shall set right in five 
 hundred year - though bear expressly to do it . 
 twice lately there have be a crash and a cloud of dust , like the 
 springing of a mine , in Tom - all - Alone 's ; and each time a house have 
 fall . these accident have make a paragraph in the newspaper 
 and have fill a bed or two in the near hospital . the gap 
 remain , and there be not unpopular lodging among the rubbish . as 
 several more house be nearly ready to go , the next crash in Tom- 
 all - Alone 's may be expect to be a good one . 
 this desirable property be in Chancery , of course . it would be an 
 insult to the discernment of any man with half an eye to tell he 
 so . whether " Tom " be the popular representative of the original 
 plaintiff or defendant in Jarndyce and Jarndyce , or whether Tom 
 live here when the suit have lay the street waste , all alone , 
 until other settler come to join he , or whether the traditional 
 title be a comprehensive name for a retreat cut off from honest 
 company and put out of the pale of hope , perhaps nobody know . 
 certainly Jo do not   know . 
 " for I do not , " say Jo , " I do not   know nothink . " 
 it must be a strange state to be like Jo ! to shuffle through the 
 street , unfamiliar with the shape , and in utter darkness as to 
 the meaning , of those mysterious symbol , so abundant over the 
 shop , and at the corner of street , and on the door , and in the 
 window ! to see people read , and to see people write , and to see 
 the postman deliver letter , and not to have the least idea of all 
 that language - to be , to every scrap of it , stone blind and dumb ! 
 it must be very puzzling to see the good company go to the 
 church on Sundays , with their book in their hand , and to think 
  for perhaps Jo do think at odd time   what do it all mean , and 
 if it mean anything to anybody , how come it that it mean nothing 
 to I ? to be hustle , and jostle , and move on ; and really to 
 feel that it would appear to be perfectly true that I have no 
 business here , or there , or anywhere ; and yet to be perplex by 
 the consideration that I be here somehow , too , and everybody 
 overlook I until I become the creature that I be ! it must be a 
 strange state , not merely to be tell that I be scarcely human   as 
 in the case of my offer myself for a witness , but to feel it of 
 my own knowledge all my life ! to see the horse , dog , and cattle 
 go by I and to know that in ignorance I belong to they and not to 
 the superior being in my shape , whose delicacy I offend ! Jo 's 
 idea of a criminal trial , or a judge , or a bishop , or a government , 
 or that inestimable jewel to he   if he only know it   the 
 Constitution , should be strange ! his whole material and immaterial 
 life be wonderfully strange ; his death , the strange thing of all . 
 Jo come out of Tom - all - Alone 's , meet the tardy morning which be 
 always late in get down there , and munch his dirty bit of 
 bread as he come along . his way lie through many street , and 
 the house not yet be open , he sit down to breakfast on the 
 door - step of the Society for the Propagation of the Gospel in 
 Foreign Parts and give it a brush when he have finish as an

 wretch , of the spiritual destitution of a coral reef in the Pacific 
 or what it cost to look up the precious soul among the coco - nut 
 and bread - fruit . 
 he go to his crossing and begin to lay it out for the day . the 
 town awake ; the great tee - totum be set up for its daily spin and 
 whirl ; all that unaccountable reading and writing , which have be 
 suspend for a few hour , recommence . Jo and the other low 
 animal get on in the unintelligible mess as they can . it be 
 market - day . the blinded oxen , over - goad , over - drive , never 
 guide , run into wrong place and be beat out , and plunge red- 
 eyed and foam at stone wall , and often sorely hurt the 
 innocent , and often sorely hurt themselves . very like Jo and his 
 order ; very , very like ! 
 a band of music come and play . Jo listen to it . so do a dog 
  - a drover 's dog , wait for his master outside a butcher 's shop , 
 and evidently think about those sheep he have have upon his mind 
 for some hour and be happily rid of . he seem perplexed 
 respect three or four , can not   remember where he leave they , look 
 up and down the street as half expect to see they astray , 
 suddenly prick up his ear and remember all about it . a 
 thoroughly vagabond dog , accustom to low company and public- 
 house ; a terrific dog to sheep , ready at a whistle to scamper over 
 their back and tear out mouthful of their wool ; but an educate , 
 improve , develop dog who have be teach his duty and know 
 how to discharge they . he and Jo listen to the music , probably 
 with much the same amount of animal satisfaction ; likewise as to 
 awakened association , aspiration , or regret , melancholy or joyful 
 reference to thing beyond the sens , they be probably upon a 
 par . but , otherwise , how far above the human listener be the 
 brute ! 
 turn that dog 's descendant wild , like Jo , and in a very few year 
 they will so degenerate that they will lose even their bark - but 
 not their bite . 
 the day change as it wear itself away and become dark and 
 drizzly . Jo fight it out at his crossing among the mud and 
 wheel , the horse , whip , and umbrella , and get but a scanty sum 
 to pay for the unsavoury shelter of Tom - all - Alone 's . Twilight 
 come on ; gas begin to start up in the shop ; the lamplighter , 
 with his ladder , run along the margin of the pavement . a wretched 
 evening be begin to close in . 
 in his chamber Mr. Tulkinghorn sit meditate an application to 
 the near magistrate to - morrow morning for a warrant . Gridley , 
 a disappointed suitor , have be here to - day and have be alarming . 
 we be not to be put in bodily fear , and that ill - condition fellow 
 shall be hold to bail again . from the ceiling , foreshorten 
 Allegory , in the person of one impossible roman upside down , point 
 with the arm of Samson   out of joint , and an odd one   obtrusively 
 toward the window . why should Mr. Tulkinghorn , for such no reason , 
 look out of window ? be the hand not always point there ? so he 
 do not look out of window . 
 and if he do , what would it be to see a woman go by ? there be 
 woman enough in the world , Mr. Tulkinghorn think - too many ; they 
 be at the bottom of all that go wrong in it , though , for the 
 matter of that , they create business for lawyer . what would it be

 be all secret . Mr. Tulkinghorn know that very well . 
 but they be not all like the woman who now leave he and his 
 house behind , between whose plain dress and her refined manner 
 there be something exceedingly inconsistent . she should be an 
 upper servant by her attire , yet in her air and step , though both 
 be hurry and assume - as far as she can assume in the muddy

 her face be veil , and still she sufficiently betray herself to 
 make more than one of those who pass she look round sharply . 
 she never turn her head . Lady or servant , she have a purpose in 
 she and can follow it . she never turn her head until she come to 
 the crossing where Jo ply with his broom . he cross with she 
 and beg . still , she do not turn her head until she have land 
 on the other side . then she slightly beckon to he and say , 
 " come here ! " 
 Jo follow she a pace or two into a quiet court .

 " I do not   know , " say Jo , star moodily at the veil , " nothink

 " come far up . " 
 " you mean about the man ? " say Jo , follow . " he as wos dead ? "

 so very ill and poor ? "

 " do he look like - not like you ? " say the woman with abhorrence . 
 " oh , not so bad as I , " say Jo . " I be a reg'lar one I be ! you 
 do not   know he , do you ? " 
 " how dare you ask I if I know he ? " 
 " no offence , my lady , " say Jo with much humility , for even he have 
 get at the suspicion of she be a lady . 
 " I be not a lady . I be a servant . " 
 " you be a jolly servant ! " say Jo without the least idea of say

 " listen and be silent . do not   talk to I , and stand far from

 account I read ? the place he write for , the place he die at , the

 Jo answer with a nod , have also nod as each other place be 
 mention . 
 " go before I and show I all those dreadful place . stop opposite 
 to each , and do not   speak to I unless I speak to you . do not   look

 Jo attend closely while the word be be speak ; tell they off

 " I do not   understand you . go on before ! I will give you more money

 CHAPTER XVII 
 Esther 's Narrative

 CHAPTER xviii 
 Lady Dedlock

 " I do , " say Mr. Boythorn with great vigour .

 " but that be take trouble , surely . and why should you take 
 trouble ? here be I , content to receive thing childishly as they 
 fall out , and I never take trouble ! I come down here , for 
 instance , and I find a mighty potentate exacting homage . very 
 well ! I say ' mighty potentate , here be my homage ! it be easy to 
 give it than to withhold it . here it be . if you have anything of 
 an agreeable nature to show I , I shall be happy to see it ; if you 
 have anything of an agreeable nature to give I , I shall be happy 
 to accept it . ' mighty potentate reply in effect , ' this be a 
 sensible fellow . I find he accord with my digestion and my 
 bilious system . he do not   impose upon I the necessity of roll 
 myself up like a hedgehog with my point outward . I expand , I 
 open , I turn my silver lining outward like Milton 's cloud , and it be 
 more agreeable to both of we . ' that be my view of such thing , 
 speak as a child ! " 
 " but suppose you go down somewhere else to - morrow , " say Mr. 
 Boythorn , " where there be the opposite of that fellow - or of this 
 fellow . how then ? " 
 " how then ? " say Mr. Skimpole with an appearance of the utmost 
 simplicity and candour . " just the same then ! I should say , ' my 
 esteemed Boythorn ' - to make you the personification of our 
 imaginary friend - ' my esteemed Boythorn , you object to the mighty 
 potentate ? very good . so do I. I take it that my business in the 
 social system be to be agreeable ; I take it that everybody 's 
 business in the social system be to be agreeable . it be a system of 
 harmony , in short . therefore if you object , I object . now , 
 excellent Boythorn , let we go to dinner ! ' " 
 " but excellent Boythorn might say , " return our host , swell and 
 grow very red , " I will be - " 
 " I understand , " say Mr. Skimpole . " very likely he would . " 
 " - if I will go to dinner ! " cry Mr. Boythorn in a violent burst 
 and stop to strike his stick upon the ground . " and he would 
 probably add , ' be there such a thing as principle , Mr. Harold 
 Skimpole ? ' " 
 " to which Harold Skimpole would reply , you know , " he return in 
 his gay manner and with his most ingenuous smile , " ' upon my life 
 I have not the least idea ! I do not   know what it be you call by 
 that name , or where it be , or who possess it . if you possess it 
 and find it comfortable , I be quite delighted and congratulate you 
 heartily . but I know nothing about it , I assure you ; for I be a 
 mere child , and I lay no claim to it , and I do not   want it ! ' so , 
 you see , excellent Boythorn and I would go to dinner after all ! " 
 this be one of many little dialogue between they which I always

 circumstance , in some violent explosion on the part of our host . 
 but he have so high a sense of his hospitable and responsible

 they all day long , that matter never go beyond this point . Mr. 
 Skimpole , who always seem quite unconscious of have be on

 exactly . 
 " enterprise and effort , " he would say to we   on his back , " be 
 delightful to I . I believe I be truly cosmopolitan . I have the

 think of adventurous spirit go to the North Pole or penetrate

 creature ask , ' what be the use of a man 's go to the North Pole ?

 I always wonder on these occasion whether he ever think of
