 CHAPTER XXI 
 the Smallweed Family 
 in a rather ill - favour and ill - savoured neighbourhood , though one 
 of its rise ground bear the name of Mount Pleasant , the Elfin 
 Smallweed , christen Bartholomew and know on the domestic hearth 
 as Bart , pass that limited portion of his time on which the 
 office and its contingency have no claim . he dwell in a little 
 narrow street , always solitary , shady , and sad , closely bricke in 
 on all side like a tomb , but where there yet linger the stump of 
 an old forest tree whose flavour be about as fresh and natural as 
 the Smallweed smack of youth . 
 there have be only one child in the Smallweed family for several 
 generation . little old man and woman there have be , but no 
 child , until Mr. Smallweed 's grandmother , now live , become weak 
 in her intellect and fall   for the first time   into a childish 
 state . with such infantine grace as a total want of observation , 
 memory , understanding , and interest , and an eternal disposition to 
 fall asleep over the fire and into it , Mr. Smallweed 's grandmother 
 have undoubtedly brighten the family . 
 Mr. Smallweed 's grandfather be likewise of the party . he be in a 
 helpless condition as to his low , and nearly so as to his upper , 
 limb , but his mind be unimpaired . it hold , as well as it ever 
 hold , the first four rule of arithmetic and a certain small 
 collection of the hard fact . in respect of ideality , 
 reverence , wonder , and other such phrenological attribute , it be 
 no bad off than it use to be . everything that Mr. Smallweed 's 
 grandfather ever put away in his mind be a grub at first , and be a 
 grub at last . in all his life he have never breed a single 
 butterfly . 
 the father of this pleasant grandfather , of the neighbourhood of 
 Mount Pleasant , be a horny - skin , two - legged , money - get 
 specie of spider who spin webs to catch unwary fly and retire 
 into hole until they be entrap . the name of this old pagan 's 
 god be Compound Interest . he live for it , marry it , die of 
 it . meet with a heavy loss in an honest little enterprise in 
 which all the loss be intend to have be on the other side , he 
 break something - something necessary to his existence , therefore it 
 could not   have be his heart - and make an end of his career . as 
 his character be not good , and he have be breed at a charity 
 school in a complete course , accord to question and answer , of 
 those ancient people the Amorites and Hittites , he be frequently 
 quote as an example of the failure of education . 
 his spirit shine through his son , to whom he have always preach of 
 " go out " early in life and whom he make a clerk in a sharp 
 scrivener 's office at twelve year old . there the young gentleman 
 improve his mind , which be of a lean and anxious character , and 
 develop the family gift , gradually elevate himself into the 
 discount profession . go out early in life and marry late , 
 as his father have do before he , he too begat a lean and anxious- 
 minded son , who in his turn , go out early in life and marrying 
 late , become the father of Bartholomew and Judith Smallweed , twin . 
 during the whole time consume in the slow growth of this family 
 tree , the house of Smallweed , always early to go out and late to 
 marry , have strengthen itself in its practical character , have 
 discard all amusement , discountenance all story - book , fairy- 
 tale , fiction , and fable , and banish all levity whatsoever . 
 hence the gratifying fact that it have have no child bear to it and 
 that the complete little man and woman whom it have produce have 
 be observe to bear a likeness to old monkey with something 
 depress on their mind . 
 at the present time , in the dark little parlour certain foot below 
 the level of the street - a grim , hard , uncouth parlour , only 
 ornament with the coarse of baize table - cover , and the hard 
 of sheet - iron tea - tray , and offer in its decorative character 
 no bad allegorical representation of Grandfather Smallweed 's mind - 
 seat in two black horsehair porter 's chair , one on each side of 
 the fire - place , the superannuate Mr. and Mrs. Smallweed while away 
 the rosy hour . on the stove be a couple of trivet for the pot 
 and kettle which it be Grandfather Smallweed 's usual occupation to 
 watch , and project from the chimney - piece between they be a sort 
 of brass gallow for roasting , which he also superintend when it 
 be in action . under the venerable Mr. Smallweed 's seat and guard 
 by his spindle leg be a drawer in his chair , report to contain 
 property to a fabulous amount . beside he be a spare cushion with 
 which he be always provide in order that he may have something to 
 throw at the venerable partner of his respected age whenever she 
 make an allusion to money - a subject on which he be particularly 
 sensitive . 
 " and where be Bart ? " Grandfather Smallweed inquire of Judy , Bart 's 
 twin sister . 
 " he an't   come in yet , " say Judy . 
 " it be his tea - time , be not   it ? " 
 " no . " 
 " how much do you mean to say it want then ? " 
 " ten minute . " 
 " hey ? " 
 " ten minute . "   Loud on the part of Judy . 
 " Ho ! " say Grandfather Smallweed . " ten minute . " 
 Grandmother Smallweed , who have be mumble and shake her head 
 at the trivet , hear figure mention , connect they with money

 ten - pound note ! " 
 Grandfather Smallweed immediately throw the cushion at she . 
 " Drat you , be quiet ! " say the good old man . 
 the effect of this act of jaculation be twofold . it not only 
 double up Mrs. Smallweed 's head against the side of her porter 's

 Judy the twin be worthy company for these associate . she be so

 knead into one would hardly make a young person of average

 Judy never own a doll , never hear of Cinderella , never play at

 strong the other way . of anything like a youthful laugh , she

 pattern of sordid age . such be Judy . 
 and her twin brother could not   wind up a top for his life . he know 
 no more of Jack the Giant Killer or of Sinbad the Sailor than he 
 know of the people in the star . he could as soon play at leap- 
 frog or at cricket as change into a cricket or a frog himself . but

 lie within the ken of Mr. Guppy . hence his admiration and his

 Judy , with a gong - like clash and clatter , set one of the sheet-

 " ha ! " he say when there be silence . " if that be her name . she

 " no ? " return the old man . " why not ? " 
 " she would want sixpence a day , and we can do it for less , " say Judy . 
 " sure ? " 
 Judy answer with a nod of deep meaning and call , as she 
 scrap the butter on the loaf with every precaution against waste 
 and cut it into slice , " you , Charley , where be you ? " timidly

 for I . make haste ! go along ! " cry Judy with a stamp upon the

 on this severe matron , as she return to her task of scrap the

 look in at the window . for whom , knife and loaf in hand , she 
 open the street - door . 
 " aye , aye , Bart ! " say Grandfather Smallweed . " here you be , hey ? " 
 " here I be , " say Bart . 
 " be along with your friend again , Bart ? " 
 small nod . 
 " dining at his expense , Bart ? " 
 small nod again . 
 " that be right . live at his expense as much as you can , and take

 CHAPTER XXII 
 Mr. Bucket 
 Allegory look pretty cool in Lincoln 's Inn Fields , though the 
 evening be hot , for both Mr. Tulkinghorn 's window be wide open , 
 and the room be lofty , gusty , and gloomy . these may not be 
 desirable characteristic when November come with fog and sleet or 
 January with ice and snow , but they have their merit in the sultry 
 long vacation weather . they enable Allegory , though it have cheek 
 like peach , and knee like bunch of blossom , and rosy 
 swelling for calf to its leg and muscle to its arm , to look 
 tolerably cool to - night . 
 Plenty of dust come in at Mr. Tulkinghorn 's window , and plenty 
 more have generate among his furniture and paper . it lie thick 
 everywhere . when a breeze from the country that have lose its way 
 take fright and make a blind hurry to rush out again , it fling 
 as much dust in the eye of Allegory as the law - or Mr. Tulkinghorn , 
 one of its trusty representative - may scatter , on occasion , in 
 the eye of the laity . 
 in his lower magazine of dust , the universal article into which 
 his paper and himself , and all his client , and all thing of

 CHAPTER XXIII 
 Esther 's Narrative 
 we come home from Mr. Boythorn 's after six pleasant week . we be 
 often in the park and in the wood and seldom pass the lodge 
 where we have take shelter without look in to speak to the 
 keeper 's wife ; but we see no more of Lady Dedlock , except at church 
 on Sundays . there be company at Chesney Wold ; and although 
 several beautiful face surround she , her face retain the same 
 influence on I as at first . I do not quite know even now whether 
 it be painful or pleasurable , whether it draw I towards she or 
 make I shrink from she . I think I admire she with a kind of 
 fear , and I know that in her presence my thought always wander 
 back , as they have do at first , to that old time of my life . 
 I have a fancy , on more than one of these Sundays , that what this

 an Appeal case

 CHAPTER XXV 
 Mrs. Snagsby see it all 
 there be disquietude in Cook 's Court , Cursitor Street . black 
 suspicion hide in that peaceful region . the mass of Cook 's 
 Courtiers be in their usual state of mind , no well and no worse ; 
 but Mr. Snagsby be change , and his little woman know it . 
 for Tom - all - Alone 's and Lincoln 's Inn Fields persist in harness 
 themselves , a pair of ungovernable courser , to the chariot of Mr. 
 Snagsby 's imagination ; and Mr. Bucket drive ; and the passenger 
 be Jo and Mr. Tulkinghorn ; and the complete equipage whirl though 
 the law - stationery business at wild speed all round the clock . 
 even in the little front kitchen where the family meal be take , 
 it rattle away at a smoking pace from the dinner - table , when Mr. 
 Snagsby pause in carve the first slice of the leg of mutton 
 bake with potato and stare at the kitchen wall . 
 Mr. Snagsby can not make out what it be that he have have to do with . 
 something be wrong somewhere , but what something , what may come of 
 it , to whom , when , and from which unthought of and unheard of 
 quarter be the puzzle of his life . his remote impression of the 
 robe and coronet , the star and garter , that sparkle through the 
 surface - dust of Mr. Tulkinghorn 's chamber ; his veneration for the 
 mystery preside over by that good and close of his customer , 
 whom all the Inns of Court , all Chancery Lane , and all the legal 
 neighbourhood agree to hold in awe ; his remembrance of Detective 
 Mr. Bucket with his forefinger and his confidential manner , 
 impossible to be evade or decline , persuade he that he be a 
 party to some dangerous secret without know what it be . and it 
 be the fearful peculiarity of this condition that , at any hour of 
 his daily life , at any opening of the shop - door , at any pull of the 
 bell , at any entrance of a messenger , or any delivery of a letter , 
 the secret may take air and fire , explode , and blow up - Mr. Bucket 
 only know whom . 
 for which reason , whenever a man unknown come into the shop   as 
 many man unknown do   and say , " be Mr. Snagsby in ? " or word to 
 that innocent effect , Mr. Snagsby 's heart knock hard at his guilty 
 breast . he undergo so much from such inquiry that when they 
 be make by boy he revenge himself by flip at their ear over 
 the counter and ask the young dog what they mean by it and why 
 they can not   speak out at once ? more impracticable man and boy 
 persist in walk into Mr. Snagsby 's sleep and terrify he with 
 unaccountable question , so that often when the cock at the little 
 dairy in Cursitor Street break out in his usual absurd way about 
 the morning , Mr. Snagsby find himself in a crisis of nightmare , 
 with his little woman shake he and say " what be the matter 
 with the man ! " 
 the little woman herself be not the least item in his difficulty . 
 to know that he be always keep a secret from she , that he have 
 under all circumstance to conceal and hold fast a tender double 
 tooth , which her sharpness be ever ready to twist out of his head , 
 give Mr. Snagsby , in her dentistical presence , much of the air of 
 a dog who have a reservation from his master and will look anywhere 
 rather than meet his eye . 
 these various sign and token , mark by the little woman , be not 
 lose upon she . they impel she to say , " Snagsby have something on 
 his mind ! " and thus suspicion get into Cook 's Court , Cursitor 
 Street . from suspicion to jealousy , Mrs. Snagsby find the road as 
 natural and short as from Cook 's Court to Chancery Lane . and thus 
 jealousy get into Cook 's Court , Cursitor Street . once there   and 
 it be always lurk thereabout , it be very active and nimble in 
 Mrs. Snagsby 's breast , prompt she to nocturnal examination of 
 Mr. Snagsby 's pocket ; to secret perusal of Mr. Snagsby 's letter ; 
 to private research in the day book and ledger , till , cash - box , 
 and iron safe ; to watching at window , listening behind door , 
 and a general putting of this and that together by the wrong end . 
 Mrs. Snagsby be so perpetually on the alert that the house become 
 ghostly with creak board and rustle garment . the ' prentice 
 think somebody may have be murder there in bygone time . 
 Guster hold certain loose atom of an idea   pick up at Tooting , 
 where they be find float among the orphan   that there be 
 bury money underneath the cellar , guard by an old man with a 
 white beard , who can not get out for seven thousand year because he 
 say the Lord 's Prayer backwards . 
 " who be Nimrod ? " Mrs. Snagsby repeatedly inquire of herself . 
 " who be that lady - that creature ? and who be that boy ? " now , 
 Nimrod be as dead as the mighty hunter whose name Mrs. Snagsby 
 have appropriate , and the lady be unproducible , she direct her 
 mental eye , for the present , with redouble vigilance to the boy . 
 " and who , " quoth Mrs. Snagsby for the thousand and first time , " be 
 that boy ? who be that - ! " and there Mrs. Snagsby be seize with 
 an inspiration . 
 he have no respect for Mr. Chadband . no , to be sure , and he 
 would not   have , of course . naturally he would not , under those 
 contagious circumstance . he be invite and appoint by Mr. 
 Chadband - why , Mrs. Snagsby hear it herself with her own ear ! - to 
 come back , and be tell where he be to go , to be address by Mr. 
 Chadband ; and he never come ! why do he never come ? because he 
 be tell not to come . who tell he not to come ? who ? ha , ha ! 
 Mrs. Snagsby see it all . 
 but happily   and Mrs. Snagsby tightly shake her head and tightly 
 smile   that boy be meet by Mr. Chadband yesterday in the street ;

 in Cook 's Court to - morrow night , " to - mor - row - night , " Mrs.

 Mrs. Snagsby screw a watchful glance on Jo as he be bring into

 Snagsby look at he . why should he do that , but that Mrs. Snagsby

 " Peace , my friend , " say Chadband , rise and wipe the oily 
 exudation from his reverend visage . " peace be with we ! my 
 friend , why with we ? because , " with his fat smile , " it can not be 
 against we , because it must be for we ; because it be not harden , 
 because it be soften ; because it do not make war like the 
 hawk , but come home unto we like the dove . therefore , my friend , 
 peace be with we ! my human boy , come forward ! " 
 stretch forth his flabby paw , Mr. Chadband lay the same on Jo 's

 reverend friend 's intention and not at all clear but that 
 something practical and painful be go to be do to he , 
 mutter , " you let I alone . I never say nothink to you . you let 
 I alone . " 
 " no , my young friend , " say Chadband smoothly , " I will not let you 
 alone . and why ? because I be a harvest - labourer , because I be a 
 toiler and a moiler , because you be deliver over unto I and be 
 become as a precious instrument in my hand . my friend , may I so 
 employ this instrument as to use it to your advantage , to your

 CHAPTER XXVI 
 Sharpshooters 
 Wintry morning , look with dull eye and sallow face upon the 
 neighbourhood of Leicester Square , find its inhabitant unwilling 
 to get out of bed . many of they be not early riser at the 
 bright of time , be bird of night who roost when the sun be 
 high and be wide awake and keen for prey when the star shine out . 
 behind dingy blind and curtain , in upper story and garret , skulk 
 more or less under false name , false hair , false title , false 
 jewellery , and false history , a colony of brigand lie in their 
 first sleep . gentleman of the green - baize road who could discourse 
 from personal experience of foreign galley and home treadmill ; 
 spy of strong government that eternally quake with weakness and 
 miserable fear , break traitor , coward , bully , gamester , 
 shuffler , swindler , and false witness ; some not unmarke by the 
 branding - iron beneath their dirty braid ; all with more cruelty in 
 they than be in Nero , and more crime than be in Newgate . for 
 howsoever bad the devil can be in fustian or smock - frock   and he 
 can be very bad in both , he be a more designing , callous , and 
 intolerable devil when he stick a pin in his shirt - front , call 
 himself a gentleman , back a card or colour , play a game or so of 
 billiard , and know a little about bill and promissory note than 
 in any other form he wear . and in such form Mr. Bucket shall find 
 he , when he will , still pervade the tributary channel of 
 Leicester Square . 
 but the wintry morning want he not and wake he not . it wake 
 Mr. George of the shoot gallery and his familiar . they arise , 
 roll up and stow away their mattress . Mr. George , have shave 
 himself before a look - glass of minute proportion , then march 
 out , bare - headed and bare - cheste , to the pump in the little yard 
 and anon come back shin with yellow soap , friction , drift 
 rain , and exceedingly cold water . as he rub himself upon a large 
 jack - towel , blow like a military sort of diver just come up , his 
 hair curl tighter and tight on his sunburnt temple the more 
 he rub it so that it look as if it never could be loosen by any 
 less coercive instrument than an iron rake or a curry - comb - as he 
 rub , and puff , and polish , and blow , turn his head from 
 side to side the more conveniently to excoriate his throat , and 
 stand with his body well bent forward to keep the wet from his 
 martial leg , Phil , on his knee light a fire , look round as if 
 it be enough washing for he to see all that do , and sufficient 
 renovation for one day to take in the superfluous health his master 
 throw off . 
 when Mr. George be dry , he go to work to brush his head with two 
 hard brush at once , to that unmerciful degree that Phil , 
 shoulder his way round the gallery in the act of sweep it , 
 wink with sympathy . this chafing over , the ornamental part of Mr. 
 George 's toilet be soon perform . he fill his pipe , light it , 
 and march up and down smoking , as his custom be , while Phil , 
 raise a powerful odour of hot roll and coffee , prepare 
 breakfast . he smoke gravely and march in slow time . perhaps 
 this morning 's pipe be devoted to the memory of Gridley in his 
 grave . 
 " and so , Phil , " say George of the shoot gallery after several 
 turn in silence , " you be dream of the country last night ? "

 out of bed .

 Phil after further consideration .

 the master resume his march , and the man resume his preparation

 be limit to the setting forth of very simple breakfast 
 requisite for two and the broiling of a rasher of bacon at the

 never bring two object at once , it take time under the 
 circumstance . at length the breakfast be ready . Phil announce

 when he have help himself , Phil follow suit , sit at the

 because it be his natural manner of eat .

 they be flat . and miste . " 
 Governor and commander be interchangeable term with Phil ,

 " that be the whole of it . " 
 " you act accord to my opinion ? "

 tell he what it be . " 
 it be that he can not have too little to do with people who be too

 CHAPTER XXVIII 
 the Ironmaster 
 Sir Leicester Dedlock have get the well , for the time being , of 
 the family gout and be once more , in a literal no less than in a 
 figurative point of view , upon his leg . he be at his place in 
 Lincolnshire ; but the water be out again on the low - lie 
 ground , and the cold and damp steal into Chesney Wold , though well 
 defend , and eke into Sir Leicester 's bone . the blaze fire of 
 faggot and coal - Dedlock timber and antediluvian forest - that blaze 
 upon the broad wide hearth and wink in the twilight on the 
 frown wood , sullen to see how tree be sacrifice , do not 
 exclude the enemy . the hot - water pipe that trail themselves all 
 over the house , the cushion door and window , and the screen 
 and curtain fail to supply the fire ' deficiency and to satisfy 
 Sir Leicester 's need . hence the fashionable intelligence proclaim 
 one morning to the listen earth that Lady Dedlock be expect 
 shortly to return to town for a few week . 
 it be a melancholy truth that even great man have their poor 
 relation . indeed great man have often more than their fair share 
 of poor relation , inasmuch as very red blood of the superior 
 quality , like inferior blood unlawfully shed , will cry aloud and 
 will be hear . Sir Leicester 's cousin , in the remotest degree , 
 be so many murder in the respect that they " will out . " among 
 whom there be cousin who be so poor that one might almost dare 
 to think it would have be the happy for they never to have be 
 plat link upon the Dedlock chain of gold , but to have be make 
 of common iron at first and do base service . 
 Service , however   with a few limited reservation , genteel but not 
 profitable , they may not do , be of the Dedlock dignity . so 
 they visit their rich cousin , and get into debt when they can , 
 and live but shabbily when they can not , and find - the woman no 
 husband , and the man no wife - and ride in borrow carriage , and 
 sit at feast that be never of their own making , and so go through 
 high life . the rich family sum have be divide by so many 
 figure , and they be the something over that nobody know what to 
 do with . 
 everybody on Sir Leicester Dedlock 's side of the question and of 
 his way of thinking would appear to be his cousin more or less . 
 from my Lord Boodle , through the Duke of Foodle , down to Noodle , 
 Sir Leicester , like a glorious spider , stretch his thread of 
 relationship . but while he be stately in the cousinship of the 
 Everybodys , he be a kind and generous man , accord to his 
 dignified way , in the cousinship of the Nobodys ; and at the present 
 time , in despite of the damp , he stay out the visit of several 
 such cousin at Chesney Wold with the constancy of a martyr . 
 of these , foremost in the front rank stand Volumnia Dedlock , a 
 young lady   of sixty   who be doubly highly related , have the 
 honour to be a poor relation , by the mother 's side , to another 
 great family . Miss Volumnia , display in early life a pretty 
 talent for cut ornament out of coloured paper , and also for 
 singing to the guitar in the spanish tongue , and propound french 
 conundrum in country house , pass the twenty year of her 
 existence between twenty and forty in a sufficiently agreeable 
 manner . Lapsing then out of date and be consider to bore 
 mankind by her vocal performance in the spanish language , she 
 retire to Bath , where she live slenderly on an annual present 
 from Sir Leicester and whence she make occasional resurrection in 
 the country house of her cousin . she have an extensive 
 acquaintance at Bath among appal old gentleman with thin leg 
 and nankeen trouser , and be of high standing in that dreary city . 
 but she be a little dread elsewhere in consequence of an 
 indiscreet profusion in the article of rouge and persistency in an 
 obsolete pearl necklace like a rosary of little bird's - egg . 
 in any country in a wholesome state , Volumnia would be a clear case 
 for the pension list . effort have be make to get she on it , and 
 when William Buffy come in , it be fully expect that her name 
 would be put down for a couple of hundred a year . but William 
 Buffy somehow discover , contrary to all expectation , that these 
 be not the time when it could be do , and this be the first 
 clear indication Sir Leicester Dedlock have convey to he that the 
 country be go to piece . 
 there be likewise the Honourable Bob Stables , who can make warm 
 mash with the skill of a veterinary surgeon and be a well shot 
 than most gamekeeper . he have be for some time particularly 
 desirous to serve his country in a post of good emolument , 
 unaccompanie by any trouble or responsibility . in a well- 
 regulate body politic this natural desire on the part of a 
 spirited young gentleman so highly connected would be speedily 
 recognize , but somehow William Buffy find when he come in that 
 these be not time in which he could manage that little matter 
 either , and this be the second indication Sir Leicester Dedlock 
 have convey to he that the country be go to piece . 
 the rest of the cousin be lady and gentleman of various age 
 and capacity , the major part amiable and sensible and likely to 
 have do well enough in life if they could have overcome their 
 cousinship ; as it be , they be almost all a little worst by it , 
 and lounge in purposeless and listless path , and seem to be quite 
 as much at a loss how to dispose of themselves as anybody else can 
 be how to dispose of they . 
 in this society , and where not , my Lady Dedlock reign supreme . 
 beautiful , elegant , accomplished , and powerful in her little world 
  for the world of fashion do not stretch all the way from pole to 
 pole , her influence in Sir Leicester 's house , however haughty and 
 indifferent her manner , be greatly to improve it and refine it . 
 the cousin , even those old cousin who be paralyse when Sir 
 Leicester marry she , do her feudal homage ; and the Honourable Bob 
 Stables daily repeat to some choose person between breakfast and 
 lunch his favourite original remark , that she be the well - groom 
 woman in the whole stud . 
 such the guest in the long drawing - room at Chesney Wold this 
 dismal night when the step on the Ghost 's Walk   inaudible here , 
 however   might be the step of a deceased cousin shut out in the 
 cold . it be near bed - time . bedroom fire blaze brightly all over 
 the house , raise ghost of grim furniture on wall and ceiling . 
 bedroom candlestick bristle on the distant table by the door , and 
 cousin yawn on othman . cousin at the piano , cousin at the

 gather round the fire . stand on one side of his own peculiar

 thought perhaps be already hop up it to bed , after a long

 " your maid , I suppose ? " 
 " no . my anything ; pet - secretary - messenger - I do not   know what . "

 I . "

 " I must beg you , my Lady , " he say while do so , " to remain a few 
 moment , for this individual of whom I speak arrive this evening

 the other cousin soon disperse , to the last cousin there . Sir

 " Sir Leicester and Lady Dedlock , as I have already apologize for

 the head of the dedlock have motion towards a sofa between 
 himself and my Lady . Mr. Rouncewell quietly take his seat there . 
 " in these busy time , when so many great undertaking be in

 Sir Leicester be content enough that the ironmaster should feel

 " quite unnecessary , Sir Leicester . now , my son be a very young

 not remain at Chesney Wold ! make it a condition ! all Sir 
 Leicester 's old misgiving relative to Wat Tyler and the people in

 " certainly not , Sir Leicester , " 
 " I be glad to hear it . " Sir Leicester very lofty indeed .

 " Willingly , Lady Dedlock . there be nothing I could desire more . " 
 address her composed face , whose intelligence , however , be too

 Sir Leicester snort a little to hear the law lay down in this 
 way , but in his honour and his love of truth , he freely , though

 want in all just respect for Chesney Wold and the family . I 
 certainly may have desire - I certainly have desire , Lady Dedlock 
  - that my mother should retire after so many year and end her day 
 with I . but as I have find that to sever this strong bond would 
 be to break her heart , I have long abandon that idea . " 
 Sir Leicester very magnificent again at the notion of Mrs. 
 Rouncewell be spirit off from her natural home to end her day 
 with an ironmaster . 
 " I have be , " proceed the visitor in a modest , clear way , " an 
 apprentice and a workman . I have live on workman 's wage , year 
 and year , and beyond a certain point have have to educate myself . 
 my wife be a foreman 's daughter , and plainly bring up . we have 
 three daughter besides this son of whom I have speak , and be 
 fortunately able to give they great advantage than we have have 
 ourselves , we have educate they well , very well . it have be one 
 of our great care and pleasure to make they worthy of any

 a little boastfulness in his fatherly tone here , as if he add in 
 his heart , " even of the Chesney Wold station . " not a little more 
 magnificence , therefore , on the part of Sir Leicester . 
 " all this be so frequent , Lady Dedlock , where I live , and among the 
 class to which I belong , that what would be generally call 
 unequal marriage be not of such rare occurrence with we as 
 elsewhere . a son will sometimes make it known to his father that 
 he have fall in love , say , with a young woman in the factory . the

 disappoint at first very possibly . it may be that he have other 
 view for his son . however , the chance be that have 
 ascertain the young woman to be of unblemished character , he will

 this girl educate for two year , ' or it may be , ' I shall place

 during which you will give I your word and honour to see she only 
 so often . if at the expiration of that time , when she have so far 
 profit by her advantage as that you may be upon a fair equality ,

 happy . ' I know of several case such as I describe , my Lady , and I

 Sir Leicester 's magnificence explode . calmly , but terribly . 
 " Mr. Rouncewell , " say Sir Leicester with his right hand in the 
 breast of his blue coat , the attitude of state in which he be

 Wold and a - " here he resist a disposition to choke , " a factory ? " 
 " I need not reply , Sir Leicester , that the two place be very 
 different ; but for the purpose of this case , I think a parallel 
 may be justly draw between they . " 
 Sir Leicester direct his majestic glance down one side of the long 
 drawing - room and up the other before he can believe that he be 
 awake . 
 " be you aware , sir , that this young woman whom my Lady - my Lady - 
 have place near her person be bring up at the village school 
 outside the gate ? " 
 " Sir Leicester , I be quite aware of it . a very good school it be , 
 and handsomely support by this family . " 
 " then , Mr. Rouncewell , " return Sir Leicester , " the application of 
 what you have say be , to I , incomprehensible . "

 ironmaster be redden a little , " that I do not regard the village

 from the village school of Chesney Wold , intact as it be this 
 minute , to the whole framework of society ; from the whole framework

 the Young Man

 CHAPTER XXX 
 Esther 's Narrative

 then she would pat I on the arm and smooth my dress , as much as to

 " Poor Mr. Woodcourt , my dear , " she would say , and always with some

 Nurse and Patient

 " Charley , " say I , " be you so cold ? " 
 " I think I be , miss , " she reply . " I do not   know what it be . I 
 can not   hold myself still . I feel so yesterday at about this same 
 time , miss . do not   be uneasy , I think I be ill . " 
 I hear Ada 's voice outside , and I hurry to the door of 
 communication between my room and our pretty sit - room , and 
 lock it . just in time , for she tap at it while my hand be 
 yet upon the key . 
 Ada call to I to let she in , but I say , " not now , my dearest . 
 go away . there be nothing the matter ; I will come to you 
 presently . " ah ! it be a long , long time before my darling girl

 Charley fall ill . in twelve hour she be very ill . I move she

 hardly ever apart , how do I learn to love it then , when I stand 
 behind the window - curtain listening and replying , but not so much 
 as look out ! how do I learn to love it afterwards , when the 
 hard time come ! 
 they put a bed for I in our sitting - room ; and by keep the door 
 wide open , I turn the two room into one , now that Ada have 
 vacate that part of the house , and keep they always fresh and 
 airy . there be not a servant in or about the house but be so 
 good that they would all most gladly have come to I at any hour of 
 the day or night without the least fear or unwillingness , but I 
 think it well to choose one worthy woman who be never to see Ada 
 and whom I could trust to come and go with all precaution . through 
 her mean I get out to take the air with my guardian when there be 
 no fear of meet Ada , and want for nothing in the way of 
 attendance , any more than in any other respect . 
 and thus poor Charley sicken and grow bad , and fall into heavy 
 danger of death , and lie severely ill for many a long round of day 
 and night . so patient she be , so uncomplaining , and inspire by 
 such a gentle fortitude that very often as I sit by Charley hold 
 her head in my arm - repose would come to she , so , when it would 
 come to she in no other attitude - I silently pray to our Father 
 in heaven that I might not forget the lesson which this little 
 sister teach I . 
 I be very sorrowful to think that Charley 's pretty look would 
 change and be disfigure , even if she recover - she be such a 
 child with her dimpled face - but that think be , for the great

 quiet in my arm when she could lie quiet nowhere else , and murmur

 use to think , how should I ever tell the two remain baby that 
 the baby who have learn of her faithful heart to be a mother to 
 they in their need be dead ! 
 there be other time when Charley know I well and talk to I , 
 tell I that she send her love to Tom and Emma and that she be 
 sure Tom would grow up to be a good man . at those time Charley 
 would speak to I of what she have read to her father as well as she 
 could to comfort he , of that young man carry out to be bury 
 who be the only son of his mother and she be a widow , of the 
 ruler 's daughter raise up by the gracious hand upon her bed of

 but of all the various time there be in Charley 's illness , there 
 be not one when she lose the gentle quality I have speak of . 
 and there be many , many when I think in the night of the last 
 high belief in the watch angel , and the last high trust in

 and Charley do not die . she flutteringly and slowly turn the

 it be a great morning when I could tell Ada all this as she stand

 in the evening I be so much bad that I resolve to prepare

 be listen to everything you say . "

 for that the poor child thank I with her whole heart . " and in

 Charley promise , and I lie down , for I be very heavy . I see the

 with her blue clear eye , God bless they , always loveliest when

 CHAPTER XXXII 
 the Appointed Time 
 it be night in Lincoln 's Inn - perplex and troublous valley of the 
 shadow of the law , where suitor generally find but little day - and

 gate be shut ; and the night - porter , a solemn warder with a mighty

 window clogged lamp like the eye of Equity , blear Argus with a

 acre of land . over which bee - like industry these benefactor of 
 their specie linger yet , though office - hour be past , that they

 whether there be two people in England less likely to come

 George and Mr. Matthew Bagnet may be very reasonably question .

 CHAPTER XXXV 
 Esther 's Narrative 
 I lay ill through several week , and the usual tenor of my life 
 become like an old remembrance . but this be not the effect of 
 time so much as of the change in all my habit make by the 
 helplessness and inaction of a sick - room . before I have be 
 confine to it many day , everything else seem to have retire 
 into a remote distance where there be little or no separation 
 between the various stage of my life which have be really divide 
 by year . in fall ill , I seem to have cross a dark lake and 
 to have leave all my experience , mingle together by the great 
 distance , on the healthy shore . 
 my housekeeping duty , though at first it cause I great anxiety 
 to think that they be unperforme , be soon as far off as the 
 old of the old duty at Greenleaf or the summer afternoon when 
 I go home from school with my portfolio under my arm , and my 
 childish shadow at my side , to my godmother 's house . I have never 
 know before how short life really be and into how small a space 
 the mind could put it . 
 while I be very ill , the way in which these division of time 
 become confused with one another distress my mind exceedingly . 
 at once a child , an eld girl , and the little woman I have be so 
 happy as , I be not only oppress by care and difficulty 
 adapt to each station , but by the great perplexity of endlessly 
 try to reconcile they . I suppose that few who have not be in 
 such a condition can quite understand what I mean or what painful 
 unrest arise from this source . 
 for the same reason I be almost afraid to hint at that time in my 
 disorder - it seem one long night , but I believe there be both 
 night and day in it - when I labour up colossal staircase , ever 
 strive to reach the top , and ever turn , as I have see a worm 
 in a garden path , by some obstruction , and labour again . I know 
 perfectly at interval , and I think vaguely at most time , that I 
 be in my bed ; and I talk with Charley , and feel her touch , and 
 know she very well ; yet I would find myself complain , " oh , more 
 of these never - end stair , Charley - more and more - pile up to 
 the sky ' , I think ! " and labour on again . 
 dare I hint at that bad time when , string together somewhere in 
 great black space , there be a flaming necklace , or ring , or starry 
 circle of some kind , of which I be one of the bead ! and when my 
 only prayer be to be take off from the rest and when it be such 
 inexplicable agony and misery to be a part of the dreadful thing ? 
 perhaps the less I say of these sick experience , the less tedious 
 and the more intelligible I shall be . I do not recall they to make 
 other unhappy or because I be now the least unhappy in remember 
 they . it may be that if we know more of such strange affliction 
 we might be the well able to alleviate their intensity . 
 the repose that succeed , the long delicious sleep , the blissful 
 rest , when in my weakness I be too calm to have any care for 
 myself and could have hear   or so I think now   that I be die , 
 with no other emotion than with a pitying love for those I leave 
 behind - this state can be perhaps more widely understood . I be in 
 this state when I first shrink from the light as it twinkle on I 
 once more , and know with a boundless joy for which no word be 
 rapturous enough that I should see again . 
 I have hear my Ada cry at the door , day and night ; I have hear 
 she call to I that I be cruel and do not love she ; I have 
 hear she pray and implore to be let in to nurse and comfort 
 I and to leave my bedside no more ; but I have only say , when I 
 could speak , " never , my sweet girl , never ! " and I have over and over 
 again remind Charley that she be to keep my darling from the 
 room whether I live or die . Charley have be true to I in that 
 time of need , and with her little hand and her great heart have keep 
 the door fast . 
 but now , my sight strengthening and the glorious light come every 
 day more fully and brightly on I , I could read the letter that my 
 dear write to I every morning and evening and could put they to my 
 lip and lie my cheek upon they with no fear of hurt she . I 
 could see my little maid , so tender and so careful , go about the 
 two room set everything in order and speak cheerfully to 
 Ada from the open window again . I could understand the stillness 
 in the house and the thoughtfulness it express on the part of all 
 those who have always be so good to I . I could weep in the 
 exquisite felicity of my heart and be as happy in my weakness as 
 ever I have be in my strength . 
 by and by my strength begin to be restore . instead of lie , with 
 so strange a calmness , watch what be do for I , as if it be 
 do for some one else whom I be quietly sorry for , I help it a 
 little , and so on to a little more and much more , until I become 
 useful to myself , and interested , and attach to life again . 
 how well I remember the pleasant afternoon when I be raise in bed 
 with pillow for the first time to enjoy a great tea - drinking with 
 Charley ! the little creature - send into the world , surely , to 
 minister to the weak and sick - be so happy , and so busy , and 
 stop so often in her preparation to lay her head upon my bosom , 
 and fondle I , and cry with joyful tear she be so glad , she be 
 so glad , that I be oblige to say , " Charley , if you go on in this 
 way , I must lie down again , my darling , for I be weak than I 
 think I be ! " so Charley become as quiet as a mouse and take she 
 bright face here and there across and across the two room , out of 
 the shade into the divine sunshine , and out of the sunshine into 
 the shade , while I watch she peacefully . when all her 
 preparation be conclude and the pretty tea - table with its 
 little delicacy to tempt I , and its white cloth , and its 
 flower , and everything so lovingly and beautifully arrange for I 
 by Ada downstairs , be ready at the bedside , I feel sure I be 
 steady enough to say something to Charley that be not new to my 
 thought . 
 first I compliment Charley on the room , and indeed it be so 
 fresh and airy , so spotless and neat , that I could scarce believe I 
 have be lie there so long . this delight Charley , and her face 
 be bright than before . 
 " yet , Charley , " say I , look round , " I miss something , surely , 
 that I be accustom to ? " 
 poor little Charley look round too and pretend to shake she 
 head as if there be nothing absent . 
 " be the picture all as they use to be ? " I ask she . 
 " every one of they , miss , " say Charley . 
 " and the furniture , Charley ? " 
 " except where I have move it about to make more room , miss . " 
 " and yet , " say I , " I miss some familiar object . ah , I know what 
 it be , Charley ! it be the look - glass . " 
 Charley get up from the table , make as if she have forget 
 something , and go into the next room ; and I hear her sob there . 
 I have think of this very often . I be now certain of it . I 
 could thank God that it be not a shock to I now . I call 
 Charley back , and when she come - at first pretend to smile , but 
 as she draw near to I , look grieve - I take she in my arm 
 and say , " it matter very little , Charley . I hope I can do 
 without my old face very well . " 
 I be presently so far advanced as to be able to sit up in a great 
 chair and even giddily to walk into the adjoining room , lean on 
 Charley . the mirror be go from its usual place in that room 
 too , but what I have to bear be none the hard to bear for that . 
 my guardian have throughout be earnest to visit I , and there be 
 now no good reason why I should deny myself that happiness . he 
 come one morning , and when he first come in , could only hold I in 
 his embrace and say , " my dear , dear girl ! " I have long know - who 
 could know well ? - what a deep fountain of affection and 
 generosity his heart be ; and be it not worth my trivial suffering 
 and change to fill such a place in it ? " oh , yes ! " I think . " he 
 have see I , and he love I well than he do ; he have see I and 
 be even fonder of I than he be before ; and what have I to mourn 
 for ! " 
 he sit down by I on the sofa , support I with his arm . for a 
 little while he sit with his hand over his face , but when he 
 remove it , fall into his usual manner . there never can have be , 
 there never can be , a pleasanter manner . 
 " my little woman , " say he , " what a sad time this have be . such 
 an inflexible little woman , too , through all ! " 
 " only for the good , guardian , " say I. 
 " for the good ? " he repeat tenderly . " of course , for the good . 
 but here have Ada and I been perfectly forlorn and miserable ; here 
 have your friend Caddy be come and go late and early ; here 
 have every one about the house be utterly lose and deject ; here 
 have even poor Rick be write - to I too - in his anxiety for 
 you ! " 
 I have read of Caddy in Ada 's letter , but not of Richard . I tell 
 he so . 
 " why , no , my dear , " he reply . " I have think it well not to 
 mention it to she . " 
 " and you speak of his writing to you , " say I , repeat his 
 emphasis . " as if it be not natural for he to do so , guardian ; 
 as if he could write to a well friend ! " 
 " he think he could , my love , " return my guardian , " and to many a 
 well . the truth be , he write to I under a sort of protest while 
 unable to write to you with any hope of an answer - write coldly , 
 haughtily , distantly , resentfully . well , dear little woman , we 
 must look forbearingly on it . he be not to blame . Jarndyce and 
 Jarndyce have warp he out of himself and pervert I in his 
 eye . I have know it do as bad deed , and bad , many a time . if 
 two angel could be concern in it , I believe it would change 
 their nature . " 
 " it have not change your , guardian . "

 CHAPTER XXXVI 
 Chesney Wold 
 Charley and I do not set off alone upon our expedition into 
 Lincolnshire . my guardian have make up his mind not to lose sight 
 of I until I be safe in Mr. Boythorn 's house , so he accompany 
 we , and we be two day upon the road . I find every breath of 
 air , and every scent , and every flower and leaf and blade of grass , 
 and every pass cloud , and everything in nature , more beautiful 
 and wonderful to I than I have ever find it yet . this be my 
 first gain from my illness . how little I have lose , when the wide 
 world be so full of delight for I . 
 my guardian intend to go back immediately , we appoint , on our 
 way down , a day when my dear girl should come . I write she a 
 letter , of which he take charge , and he leave we within half an hour 
 of our arrival at our destination , on a delightful evening in the 
 early summer - time . 
 if a good fairy have build the house for I with a wave of her wand , 
 and I have be a princess and she favour god - child , I could not 
 have be more consider in it . so many preparation be make 
 for I and such an endearing remembrance be show of all my little 
 taste and liking that I could have sit down , overcome , a dozen

 that , however , by show they all to Charley instead . Charley 's 
 delight calm mine ; and after we have have a walk in the garden , and 
 Charley have exhaust her whole vocabulary of admire expression , 
 I be as tranquilly happy as I ought to have be . it be a great 
 comfort to be able to say to myself after tea , " Esther , my dear , I 
 think you be quite sensible enough to sit down now and write a 
 note of thank to your host . " he have leave a note of welcome for 
 I , as sunny as his own face , and have confide his bird to my care , 
 which I know to be his high mark of confidence . accordingly I 
 write a little note to he in London , tell he how all his 
 favourite plant and tree be look , and how the most 
 astonishing of bird have chirp the honour of the house to I in

 roost in the usual corner of his cage , but whether dream or no I 
 could not report . my note finish and send off to the post , I 
 make myself very busy in unpack and arrange ; and I send 
 Charley to bed in good time and tell she I should want she no more 
 that night . 
 for I have not yet look in the glass and have never ask to have 
 my own restore to I . I know this to be a weakness which must be 
 overcome , but I have always say to myself that I would begin afresh 
 when I get to where I now be . therefore I have want to be alone , 
 and therefore I say , now alone , in my own room , " Esther , if you 
 be to be happy , if you be to have any right to pray to be true- 
 hearted , you must keep your word , my dear . " I be quite resolve 
 to keep it , but I sit down for a little while first to reflect upon 
 all my blessing . and then I say my prayer and think a little 
 more . 
 my hair have not be cut off , though it have be in danger more 
 than once . it be long and thick . I let it down , and shake it 
 out , and go up to the glass upon the dressing - table . there be a 
 little muslin curtain draw across it . I draw it back and stand

 could see nothing else . then I put my hair aside and look at the

 look at I . I be very much change - oh , very , very much . at

 my hand before it and start back but for the encouragement I 
 have mention . very soon it become more familiar , and then I know

 it be not like what I have expect , but I have expect nothing 
 definite , and I dare say anything definite would have surprise I . 
 I have never be a beauty and have never think myself one , but I 
 have be very different from this . it be all go now . Heaven 
 be so good to I that I could let it go with a few not bitter 
 tear and could stand there arrange my hair for the night quite 
 thankfully . 
 one thing trouble I , and I consider it for a long time before I 
 go to sleep . I have keep Mr. Woodcourt 's flower . when they be 
 wither I have dry they and put they in a book that I be fond 
 of . nobody know this , not even Ada . I be doubtful whether I have

 he . at last I come to the conclusion that I might keep they if I 
 treasure they only as a remembrance of what be irrevocably past 
 and go , never to be look back on any more , in any other light . 
 I hope this may not seem trivial . I be very much in earnest . 
 I take care to be up early in the morning and to be before the 
 glass when Charley come in on tiptoe . 
 " dear , dear , miss ! " cry Charley , start . " be that you ? " 
 " yes , Charley , " say I , quietly put up my hair . " and I be very 
 well indeed , and very happy . " 
 I see it be a weight off Charley 's mind , but it be a great 
 weight off mine . I know the bad now and be compose to it . I 
 shall not conceal , as I go on , the weakness I could not quite 
 conquer , but they always pass from I soon and the happy frame 
 of mind stay by I faithfully . 
 wish to be fully re - establish in my strength and my good 
 spirit before Ada come , I now lay down a little series of plan 
 with Charley for be in the fresh air all day long . we be to 
 be out before breakfast , and be to dine early , and be to be out

 tea , and be to go to rest betime , and be to climb every hill 
 and explore every road , lane , and field in the neighbourhood . as 
 to restorative and strengthen delicacy , Mr. Boythorn 's good

 CHAPTER XXXVII 
 Jarndyce and Jarndyce 
 if the secret I have to keep have be mine , I must have confide it

 the difficulty that I feel in be quite compose that first

 CHAPTER XXXVIII 
 a Struggle 
 when our time come for return to Bleak House again , we be

 CHAPTER XL 
 National and Domestic 
 England have be in a dreadful state for some week . Lord Coodle 
 would go out , Sir Thomas Doodle would not   come in , and there be 
 nobody in Great Britain   to speak of   except Coodle and Doodle , 
 there have be no government . it be a mercy that the hostile

 difference should never induce he to withhold from it the tribute

 Leicester Dedlock   to weather the storm ; and the marvellous part of

 nephews , all his male cousin , and all his brother - in - law . so 
 there be hope for the old ship yet .

 CHAPTER XLIII 
 Esther 's Narrative 
 it matter little now how much I think of my living mother who 
 have tell I evermore to consider she dead . I could not venture to 
 approach she or to communicate with she in writing , for my sense of

 my fear of increase it . know that my mere existence as a 
 living creature be an unforeseen danger in her way , I could not 
 always conquer that terror of myself which have seize I when I 
 first know the secret . at no time do I dare to utter her name . I

 anywhere , when I be present , take that direction , as it sometimes

 it matter little now how often I recall the tone of my mother 's

 CHAPTER XLIV 
 the Letter and the Answer

 " and so Esther , my dear , you be happy for life . happy with your

 perhaps the name bring they to my remembrance . the dried remain

 they be in a book , and it happen to be in the next room - our

 on enter the breakfast - room next morning , I find my guardian

 so , on the next morning , and the next , and for at least a week ,

 I think then , grow uneasy , that I ought to write an answer . I

 much mighty speech - making there have be , both in and out of

 but he have his revenge . even the wind be his messenger , and

 it be a moot point whether Tom - all - Alone 's be uglier by day or by

 a brown sunburnt gentleman , who appear in some inaptitude for

 CHAPTER XLVII 
 Jo 's will 
 as Allan Woodcourt and Jo procee along the street where the high 
 church spire and the distance be so near and clear in the 
 morning light that the city itself seem renew by rest , Allan 
 revolve in his mind how and where he shall bestow his companion . 
 " it surely be a strange fact , " he consider , " that in the heart of 
 a civilized world this creature in human form should be more 
 difficult to dispose of than an unowned dog . " but it be none the 
 less a fact because of its strangeness , and the difficulty remain . 
 at first he look behind he often to assure himself that Jo be 
 still really follow . but look where he will , he still behold 
 he close to the opposite house , make his way with his wary hand 
 from brick to brick and from door to door , and often , as he creep 
 along , glance over at he watchfully . soon satisfied that the 
 last thing in his thought be to give he the slip , Allan go on , 
 consider with a less divided attention what he shall do . 
 a breakfast - stall at a street - corner suggest the first thing to be 
 do . he stop there , look round , and beckon Jo . Jo cross and 
 come halt and shuffle up , slowly scoop the knuckle of his 
 right hand round and round in the hollowed palm of his left , 
 knead dirt with a natural pestle and mortar . what be a dainty 
 repast to Jo be then set before he , and he begin to gulp the

 he in all direction as he eat and drink , like a scared animal . 
 but he be so sick and miserable that even hunger have abandon he . 
 " I think I be amost a - starvin , sir , " say Jo , soon put down 
 his food , " but I do not   know nothink - not even that . I do not   care 
 for eat wittle nor yet for drink on them . " and Jo stand 
 shivering and look at the breakfast wonderingly . 
 Allan Woodcourt lay his hand upon his pulse and on his chest . 
 " draw breath , Jo ! " " it draw , " say Jo , " as heavy as a cart . " he 
 might add , " and rattle like it , " but he only mutter , " I be a- 
 move on , sir . " 
 Allan look about for an apothecary 's shop . there be none at hand ,

 begin to revive almost as soon as it pass his lip . " we may 
 repeat that dose , Jo , " observe Allan after watch he with his 
 attentive face . " so ! now we will take five minute ' rest , and 
 then go on again . " 
 leave the boy sit on the bench of the breakfast - stall , with 
 his back against an iron railing , Allan Woodcourt pace up and down

 without appear to watch he . it require no discernment to

 intend to refer his difficulty in find a temporary place of

 " my dear physician ! " cry Miss Flite . " my meritorious , 
 distinguish , honourable officer ! " she use some odd expression ,

 than it often be . Allan , very patient with she , wait until she have

 " where can I lodge he hereabout for the present ? now , you have a 
 fund of knowledge and good sense and can advise I . "

 sure ! of course ! my dear physician ! General George will help we

 it be hopeless to ask for any information about General George , and 
 would be , though Miss Flite have not akeady run upstairs to put on

 from the exterior of George 's Shooting Gallery , and the long entry ,

 stride towards they in his morning exercise with his pipe in his

 " your servant , sir , " say Mr. George with a military salute . Good-

 " excuse I , sir . a sailor , I believe ? " say Mr. George . 
 " I be proud to find I have the air of one , " return Allan ; " but I 
 be only a sea - go doctor . " 
 " indeed , sir ! I should have think you be a regular blue - jacket 
 myself . " 
 Allan hop Mr. George will forgive his intrusion the more readily 
 on that account , and particularly that he will not lay aside his 
 pipe , which , in his politeness , he have testify some intention of 
 do . " you be very good , sir , " return the trooper . " as I know

 " the same man . "

 " now , I wish Mr. Jarndyce and Miss Summerson at least to know that

 as he put the question , he become aware of a dirty - face little

 moment 's notice . however , sir , such as the place be , and so long

 with a comprehensive wave of his pipe , Mr. George place the whole 
 building at his visitor 's disposal . 
 " I take it for grant , sir , " he add , " you be one of the 
 medical staff , that there be no present infection about this 
 unfortunate subject ? " 
 Allan be quite sure of it . 
 " because , sir , " say Mr. George , shake his head sorrowfully , " we 
 have have enough of that . " 
 his tone be no less sorrowfully echo by his new acquaintance .

 CHAPTER XLVIII 
 closing in 
 the place in Lincolnshire have shut its many eye again , and the

 CHAPTER XLIX 
 Dutiful Friendship 
 a great annual occasion have come round in the establishment of Mr. 
 Matthew Bagnet , otherwise Lignum Vitae , ex - artilleryman and present 
 bassoon - player . an occasion of feasting and festival . the 
 celebration of a birthday in the family . 
 it be not Mr. Bagnet 's birthday . Mr. Bagnet merely distinguish 
 that epoch in the musical instrument business by kiss the 
 child with an extra smack before breakfast , smoke an 
 additional pipe after dinner , and wonder towards evening what 
 his poor old mother be think about it - a subject of infinite 
 speculation , and render so by his mother have depart this 
 life twenty year . some man rarely revert to their father , but 
 seem , in the bank - book of their remembrance , to have transfer 
 all the stock of filial affection into their mother 's name . 
 Mr. Bagnet be one of these . perhaps his exalted appreciation 
 of the merit of the old girl cause he usually to make the 
 noun - substantive " goodness " of the feminine gender . 
 it be not the birthday of one of the three child . those 
 occasion be keep with some mark of distinction , but they rarely 
 overleap the bound of happy return and a pudding . on young 
 Woolwich 's last birthday , Mr. Bagnet certainly do , after observe 
 on his growth and general advancement , proceed , in a moment of 
 profound reflection on the change work by time , to examine he 
 in the catechism , accomplish with extreme accuracy the question 
 number one and two , " what be your name ? " and " who give you that 
 name ? " but there fail in the exact precision of his memory and 
 substituting for number three the question " and how do you like 
 that name ? " which he propound with a sense of its importance , in 
 itself so edify and improve as to give it quite an orthodox 
 air . this , however , be a speciality on that particular birthday , 
 and not a general solemnity . 
 it be the old girl 's birthday , and that be the great holiday and 
 reddest - letter day in Mr. Bagnet 's calendar . the auspicious event 
 be always commemorate accord to certain form settle and 
 prescribe by Mr. Bagnet some year since . Mr. Bagnet , be 
 deeply convinced that to have a pair of fowl for dinner be to 
 attain the high pitch of imperial luxury , invariably go forth 
 himself very early in the morning of this day to buy a pair ; he be , 
 as invariably , take in by the vendor and instal in the 
 possession of the old inhabitant of any coop in Europe . 
 return with these triumph of toughness tie up in a clean blue 
 and white cotton handkerchief   essential to the arrangement , he 
 in a casual manner invite Mrs. Bagnet to declare at breakfast what 
 she would like for dinner . Mrs. Bagnet , by a coincidence never 
 know to fail , reply fowl , Mr. Bagnet instantly produce his 
 bundle from a place of concealment amidst general amazement and

 CHAPTER L 
 Esther 's Narrative 
 it happen that when I come home from Deal I find a note from 
 Caddy Jellyby   as we always continue to call she , inform I 
 that her health , which have be for some time very delicate , be 
 bad and that she would be more glad than she could tell I if I 
 would go to see she . it be a note of a few line , write from 
 the couch on which she lie and enclose to I in another from her 
 husband , in which he second her entreaty with much solicitude . 
 Caddy be now the mother , and I the godmother , of such a poor 
 little baby - such a tiny old - faced mite , with a countenance that

 CHAPTER LI 
 Enlightened 
 when Mr. Woodcourt arrive in London , he go , that very same day ,

 CHAPTER LII 
 Obstinacy

 Mr. Bucket and his fat forefinger be much in consultation together

 CHAPTER LV 
 Flight 
 Inspector Bucket of the Detective have not yet strike his great 
 blow , as just now chronicle , but be yet refresh himself with 
 sleep preparatory to his field - day , when through the night and 
 along the freezing wintry road a chaise and pair come out of 
 Lincolnshire , make its way towards London . 
 railroad shall soon traverse all this country , and with a rattle 
 and a glare the engine and train shall shoot like a meteor over the 
 wide night - landscape , turn the moon paler ; but as yet such 
 thing be non - existent in these part , though not wholly 
 unexpected . preparation be afoot , measurement be make , ground 
 be stake out . bridge be begin , and their not yet united pier 
 desolately look at one another over road and stream like brick 
 and mortar couple with an obstacle to their union ; fragment of 
 embankment be throw up and leave as precipice with torrent of 
 rusty cart and barrow tumble over they ; tripod of tall pole 
 appear on hilltop , where there be rumour of tunnel ; everything 
 look chaotic and abandon in full hopelessness . along the 
 freeze road , and through the night , the post - chaise make its 
 way without a railroad on its mind . 
 Mrs. Rouncewell , so many year housekeeper at Chesney Wold , sit 
 within the chaise ; and by her side sit Mrs. Bagnet with her grey 
 cloak and umbrella . the old girl would prefer the bar in front , as 
 be expose to the weather and a primitive sort of perch more in 
 accordance with her usual course of travel , but Mrs. Rouncewell 
 be too thoughtful of her comfort to admit of she propose it . the 
 old lady can not make enough of the old girl . she sit , in her 
 stately manner , hold her hand , and regardless of its roughness , 
 put it often to her lip . " you be a mother , my dear soul , " say 
 she many time , " and you find out my George 's mother ! " 
 " why , George , " return Mrs. Bagnet , " be always free with I , 
 ma'am , and when he say at our house to my Woolwich that of all the 
 thing my Woolwich could have to think of when he grow to be a man , 
 the comfortable would be that he have never bring a sorrowful 
 line into his mother 's face or turn a hair of her head grey , then 
 I feel sure , from his way , that something fresh have bring his own 
 mother into his mind . I have often know he say to I , in past 
 time , that he have behave bad to she . " 
 " never , my dear ! " return Mrs. Rouncewell , burst into tear . 
 " my blessing on he , never ! he be always fond of I , and love 
 to I , be my George ! but he have a bold spirit , and he run a 
 little wild and go for a soldier . and I know he wait at first , 
 in let we know about himself , till he should rise to be an 
 officer ; and when he do not   rise , I know he consider himself 
 beneath we , and would not   be a disgrace to we . for he have a lion 
 heart , have my George , always from a baby ! " 
 the old lady 's hand stray about she as of yore , while she recall , 
 all in a tremble , what a likely lad , what a fine lad , what a gay 
 good - humour clever lad he be ; how they all take to he down at 
 Chesney Wold ; how Sir Leicester take to he when he be a young 
 gentleman ; how the dog take to he ; how even the people who have 
 be angry with he forgave he the moment he be gone , poor boy . 
 and now to see he after all , and in a prison too ! and the broad 
 stomacher heave , and the quaint upright old - fashioned figure bend 
 under its load of affectionate distress . 
 Mrs. Bagnet , with the instinctive skill of a good warm heart , 
 leave the old housekeeper to her emotion for a little while - not 
 without pass the back of her hand across her own motherly eye - 
 and presently chirp up in her cheery manner , " so I say to George 
 when I go to call he in to tea   he pretend to be smoke his 
 pipe outside , ' what ail you this afternoon , George , for gracious 
 sake ? I have see all sort , and I have see you pretty often in 
 season and out of season , abroad and at home , and I never see you 
 so melancholy penitent . ' ' why , Mrs. Bagnet , ' say George , ' it be 
 because I be melancholy and penitent both , this afternoon , that you 
 see I so . ' ' what have you do , old fellow ? ' I say . ' why , Mrs. 
 Bagnet , ' say George , shake his head , ' what I have do have be 
 do this many a long year , and be good not try to be undo now . 
 if I ever get to heaven it will not   be for be a good son to a 
 widow mother ; I say no more . ' now , ma'am , when George say to I 
 that it be well not try to be undo now , I have my thought as I 
 have often have before , and I draw it out of George how he come to 
 have such thing on he that afternoon . then George tell I that 
 he have see by chance , at the lawyer 's office , a fine old lady that 
 have bring his mother plain before he , and he run on about that 
 old lady till he quite forget himself and paint her picture to I 
 as she use to be , year upon year back . so I say to George when 
 he have do , who be this old lady he have see ? and George tell I 
 it be Mrs. Rouncewell , housekeeper for more than half a century to 
 the Dedlock family down at Chesney Wold in Lincolnshire . George

 CHAPTER LVI 
 Pursuit 
 Impassive , as behove its high breeding , the Dedlock town house 
 stare at the other house in the street of dismal grandeur and 
 give no outward sign of anything go wrong within . carriage

 fascinating creature look like Death and the Lady fuse together , 
 dazzle the eye of man . forth from the frigid mew come easily

 Mr. Jarndyce , the only person up in the house , be just go to
