 CHAPTER LVII 
 Esther 's Narrative 
 I have go to bed and fall asleep when my guardian knock at the 
 door of my room and beg I to get up directly . on my hurrying 
 to speak to he and learn what have happen , he tell I , after a 
 word or two of preparation , that there have be a discovery at Sir 
 Leicester Dedlock 's . that my mother have flee , that a person be 
 now at our door who be empower to convey to she the full 
 assurance of affectionate protection and forgiveness if he could 
 possibly find she , and that I be seek for to accompany he in 
 the hope that my entreaty might prevail upon she if his fail . 
 something to this general purpose I make out , but I be throw into 
 such a tumult of alarm , and hurry and distress , that in spite of 
 every effort I could make to subdue my agitation , I do not seem , 
 to myself , fully to recover my right mind until hour have pass . 
 but I dress and wrap up expeditiously without wake Charley 
 or any one and go down to Mr. Bucket , who be the person 
 entrust with the secret . in take I to he my guardian tell I 
 this , and also explain how it be that he have come to think of 
 I . Mr. Bucket , in a low voice , by the light of my guardian 's 
 candle , read to I in the hall a letter that my mother have leave 
 upon her table ; and I suppose within ten minute of my have be 
 arouse I be sit beside he , roll swiftly through the 
 street . 
 his manner be very keen , and yet considerate when he explain to 
 I that a great deal might depend on my be able to answer , 
 without confusion , a few question that he wish to ask I . these 
 be , chiefly , whether I have have much communication with my mother 
  to whom he only refer as Lady Dedlock , when and where I have 
 speak with her last , and how she have become possess of my 
 handkerchief . when I have satisfy he on these point , he ask 
 I particularly to consider - take time to think - whether within 
 my knowledge there be any one , no matter where , in whom she might 
 be at all likely to confide under circumstance of the last 
 necessity . I could think of no one but my guardian . but by and by 
 I mention Mr. Boythorn . he come into my mind as connect with 
 his old chivalrous manner of mention my mother 's name and with 
 what my guardian have inform I of his engagement to her sister 
 and his unconscious connexion with her unhappy story . 
 my companion have stop the driver while we hold this 
 conversation , that we might the well hear each other . he now 
 tell he to go on again and say to I , after consider within 
 himself for a few moment , that he have make up his mind how to 
 proceed . he be quite willing to tell I what his plan be , but I 
 do not feel clear enough to understand it . 
 we have not drive very far from our lodging when we stop in a 
 by - street at a public - look place light up with gas . Mr. 
 Bucket take I in and sit I in an arm - chair by a bright fire . it 
 be now past one , as I see by the clock against the wall . two 
 police officer , look in their perfectly neat uniform not at all 
 like people who be up all night , be quietly write at a desk ; 
 and the place seem very quiet altogether , except for some beating 
 and call out at distant door underground , to which nobody pay 
 any attention . 
 a third man in uniform , whom Mr. Bucket call and to whom he 
 whisper his instruction , go out ; and then the two other 
 advise together while one write from Mr. Bucket 's subdued 
 dictation . it be a description of my mother that they be busy 
 with , for Mr. Bucket bring it to I when it be do and read it 
 in a whisper . it be very accurate indeed . 
 the second officer , who have attend to it closely , then copy it 
 out and call in another man in uniform   there be several in an 
 outer room , who take it up and go away with it . all this be 
 do with the great dispatch and without the waste of a moment ; 
 yet nobody be at all hurry . as soon as the paper be send out 
 upon its travel , the two officer resume their former quiet work 
 of write with neatness and care . Mr. Bucket thoughtfully come 
 and warm the sol of his boot , first one and then the other , at 
 the fire . 
 " be you well wrap up , Miss Summerson ? " he ask I as his eye 
 meet mine . " it be a desperate sharp night for a young lady to be out 
 in . " 
 I tell he I care for no weather and be warmly clothe . 
 " it may be a long job , " he observe ; " but so that it end well , 
 never mind , miss . " 
 " I pray to heaven it may end well ! " say I. 
 he nod comfortingly . " you see , whatever you do , do not   you go 
 and fret yourself . you keep yourself cool and equal for anything 
 that may happen , and it will be the well for you , the well for 
 I , the well for Lady Dedlock , and the well for Sir Leicester 
 Dedlock , Baronet . " 
 he be really very kind and gentle , and as he stand before the fire 
 warm his boot and rub his face with his forefinger , I feel 
 a confidence in his sagacity which reassure I . it be not yet a 
 quarter to two when I hear horse ' foot and wheel outside . " now , 
 Miss Summerson , " say he , " we be off , if you please ! " 
 he give I his arm , and the two officer courteously bow I out , 
 and we find at the door a phaeton or barouche with a postilion and 
 post horse . Mr. Bucket hand I in and take his own seat on the 
 box . the man in uniform whom he have send to fetch this equipage 
 then hand he up a dark lantern at his request , and when he have 
 give a few direction to the driver , we rattle away . 
 I be far from sure that I be not in a dream . we rattle with 
 great rapidity through such a labyrinth of street that I soon lose 
 all idea where we be , except that we have cross and re - cross 
 the river , and still seem to be traverse a low - lie , 
 waterside , dense neighbourhood of narrow thoroughfare chequer by 
 dock and basin , high pile of warehouse , swing - bridge , and 
 mast of ship . at length we stop at the corner of a little 
 slimy turning , which the wind from the river , rush up it , do 
 not purify ; and I see my companion , by the light of his lantern , in 
 conference with several man who look like a mixture of police and 
 sailor . against the mouldering wall by which they stand , there 
 be a bill , on which I could discern the word , " find drown " ; 
 and this and an inscription about drag possess I with the awful 
 suspicion shadow forth in our visit to that place . 
 I have no need to remind myself that I be not there by the 
 indulgence of any feeling of mine to increase the difficulty of 
 the search , or to lessen its hope , or enhance its delay . I 
 remain quiet , but what I suffer in that dreadful spot I never 
 can forget . and still it be like the horror of a dream . a man 
 yet dark and muddy , in long swollen sodden boot and a hat like 
 they , be call out of a boat and whisper with Mr. Bucket , who 
 go away with he down some slippery step - as if to look at 
 something secret that he have to show . they come back , wipe their 
 hand upon their coat , after turn over something wet ; but thank 
 God it be not what I fear ! 
 after some further conference , Mr. Bucket   whom everybody seem to 
 know and defer to   go in with the other at a door and leave I in 
 the carriage , while the driver walk up and down by his horse to 
 warm himself . the tide be come in , as I judge from the sound 
 it make , and I could hear it break at the end of the alley with a 
 little rush towards I . it never do so - and I think it do so , 
 hundred of time , in what can have be at the most a quarter of 
 an hour , and probably be less - but the thought shudder through 
 I that it would cast my mother at the horse ' foot . 
 Mr. Bucket come out again , exhort the other to be vigilant , 
 darken his lantern , and once more take his seat . " do not   you be 
 alarm , Miss Summerson , on account of our come down here , " he 
 say , turn to I . " I only want to have everything in train and 
 to know that it be in train by look after it myself . get on , my 
 lad ! " 
 we appear to retrace the way we have come . not that I have take 
 note of any particular object in my perturbed state of mind , but 
 judge from the general character of the street . we call at 
 another office or station for a minute and cross the river again . 
 during the whole of this time , and during the whole search , my 
 companion , wrap up on the box , never relax in his vigilance a 
 single moment ; but when we cross the bridge he seem , if 
 possible , to be more on the alert than before . he stand up to look 
 over the parapet , he alight and go back after a shadowy female 
 figure that flit past we , and he gaze into the profound black 
 pit of water with a face that make my heart die within I . the 
 river have a fearful look , so overcast and secret , creep away so 
 fast between the low flat line of shore - so heavy with indistinct 
 and awful shape , both of substance and shadow ; so death - like and 
 mysterious . I have see it many time since then , by sunlight and 
 by moonlight , but never free from the impression of that journey . 
 in my memory the light upon the bridge be always burn dim , the 
 cutting wind be eddying round the homeless woman whom we pass , the 
 monotonous wheel be whirl on , and the light of the carriage- 
 lamp reflect back look palely in upon I - a face rise out of 
 the dreaded water . 
 clattering and clatter through the empty street , we come at 
 length from the pavement on to dark smooth road and begin to leave 
 the house behind we . after a while I recognize the familiar way 
 to Saint Albans . at Barnet fresh horse be ready for we , and we 
 change and go on . it be very cold indeed , and the open country 
 be white with snow , though none be fall then . 
 " an old acquaintance of your , this road , Miss Summerson , " say Mr. 
 Bucket cheerfully . 
 " yes , " I return . " have you gather any intelligence ? " 
 " none that can be quite depended on as yet , " he answer , " but it be 
 early time as yet . " 
 he have go into every late or early public - house where there be a 
 light   they be not a few at that time , the road be then much 
 frequent by drover   and have get down to talk to the turnpike- 
 keeper . I have hear he order drink , and chink money , and 
 make himself agreeable and merry everywhere ; but whenever he take 
 his seat upon the box again , his face resume its watchful steady 
 look , and he always say to the driver in the same business tone , 
 " get on , my lad ! " 
 with all these stoppage , it be between five and six o'clock and 
 we be yet a few mile short of Saint Albans when he come out of 
 one of these house and hand I in a cup of tea . 
 " drink it , Miss Summerson , it will do you good . you be begin to 
 get more yourself now , be not   you ? " 
 I thank he and say I hop so . 
 " you be what you may call stunned at first , " he return ; " and 
 Lord , no wonder ! do not   speak loud , my dear . it be all right . 
 she be on ahead . " 
 I do not   know what joyful exclamation I make or be go to make , 
 but he put up his finger and I stop myself . 
 " pass through here on foot this evening about eight or nine . I 
 hear of she first at the archway toll , over at Highgate , but 
 could not   make quite sure . trace she all along , on and off . 
 pick she up at one place , and drop she at another ; but she be 
 before we now , safe . take hold of this cup and saucer , ostler . 
 now , if you be not   bring up to the butter trade , look out and see 
 if you can catch half a crown in your t'other hand . one , two , 
 three , and there you be ! now , my lad , try a gallop ! " 
 we be soon in Saint Albans and alight a little before day , when 
 I be just begin to arrange and comprehend the occurrence of 
 the night and really to believe that they be not a dream . 
 leave the carriage at the posting - house and order fresh horse 
 to be ready , my companion give I his arm , and we go towards 
 home . 
 " as this be your regular abode , Miss Summerson , you see , " he 
 observe , " I should like to know whether you have   be ask for by 
 any stranger answer the description , or whether Mr. Jarndyce 
 have . I do not   much expect it , but it might be . " 
 as we ascend the hill , he look about he with a sharp eye - the 
 day be now break - and remind I that I have come down it one 
 night , as I have reason for remembering , with my little servant and 
 poor Jo , whom he call Toughey . 
 I wonder how he know that . 
 " when you pass a man upon the road , just yonder , you know , " say 
 Mr. Bucket . 
 yes , I remember that too , very well . 
 " that be I , " say Mr. Bucket . 
 see my surprise , he go on , " I drive down in a gig that 
 afternoon to look after that boy . you might have hear my wheel 
 when you come out to look after he yourself , for I be aware of 
 you and your little maid go up when I be walk the horse 
 down . make an inquiry or two about he in the town , I soon hear 
 what company he be in and be come among the brick - field to

 CHAPTER lviii 
 a Wintry Day and Night 
 still impassive , as behove its breeding , the Dedlock town house 
 carry itself as usual towards the street of dismal grandeur . 
 there be powdered head from time to time in the little window of 
 the hall , look out at the untaxed powder fall all day from

 turn itself exotically to the great hall fire from the nipping 
 weather out of door . it be give out that my Lady have go down 
 into Lincolnshire , but be expect to return presently . 
 Rumour , busy overmuch , however , will not go down into Lincolnshire . 
 it persist in flit and chatter about town . it know that 
 that poor unfortunate man , Sir Leicester , have be sadly use . it 
 hear , my dear child , all sort of shocking thing . it make the 
 world of five mile round quite merry . not to know that there be 
 something wrong at the Dedlocks ' be to augur yourself unknown . one 
 of the peachy - cheek charmer with the skeleton throat be already 
 apprise of all the principal circumstance that will come out 
 before the Lords on Sir Leicester 's application for a bill of 
 divorce . 
 at Blaze and Sparkle 's the jeweller and at Sheen and Gloss 's the 
 mercer , it be and will be for several hour the topic of the age ,

 albeit so loftily inscrutable , be as nicely weigh and measure 
 there as any other article of the stock - in - trade , be perfectly 
 understand in this new fashion by the raw hand behind the counter . 
 " our people , Mr. Jones , " say Blaze and Sparkle to the hand in 
 question on engage he , " our people , sir , be sheep - mere sheep . 
 where two or three marked one go , all the rest follow . keep those 
 two or three in your eye , Mr. Jones , and you have the flock . " so , 
 likewise , Sheen and Gloss to their Jones , in reference to know 
 where to have the fashionable people and how to bring what they 
  Sheen and Gloss   choose into fashion . on similar unerring 
 principle , Mr. Sladdery the librarian , and indeed the great 
 farmer of gorgeous sheep , admit this very day , " why yes , sir , 
 there certainly be report concern Lady Dedlock , very current 
 indeed among my high connexion , sir . you see , my high connexion 
 must talk about something , sir ; and it be only to get a subject 
 into vogue with one or two lady I could name to make it go down

 they have do of themselves in this case through know Lady 
 Dedlock and be perhaps a little innocently jealous of she too ,

 CHAPTER LIX 
 Esther 's Narrative 
 it be three o'clock in the morning when the house outside London 
 do at last begin to exclude the country and to close we in with 
 street . we have make our way along road in a far bad condition 
 than when we have traverse they by daylight , both the fall and the 
 thaw have last ever since ; but the energy of my companion never 
 slacken . it have only be , as I think , of less assistance than 
 the horse in get we on , and it have often aid they . they have 
 stop exhausted half - way up hill , they have be drive through 
 stream of turbulent water , they have slip down and become 
 entangle with the harness ; but he and his little lantern have be 
 always ready , and when the mishap be set right , I have never hear 
 any variation in his cool , " get on , my lad ! " 
 the steadiness and confidence with which he have direct our 
 journey back I could not account for . never waver , he never 
 even stop to make an inquiry until we be within a few mile of 
 London . a very few word , here and there , be then enough for 
 he ; and thus we come , at between three and four o'clock in the 
 morning , into Islington . 
 I will not dwell on the suspense and anxiety with which I reflect 
 all this time that we be leave my mother far and far 
 behind every minute . I think I have some strong hope that he must 
 be right and could not fail to have a satisfactory object in 
 follow this woman , but I torment myself with question it 
 and discuss it during the whole journey . what be to ensue when 
 we find she and what could compensate we for this loss of time 
 be question also that I could not possibly dismiss ; my mind be 
 quite torture by long dwelling on such reflection when we 
 stop . 
 we stop in a high - street where there be a coach - stand . my 
 companion pay our two driver , who be as completely cover with 
 splash as if they have be drag along the road like the 
 carriage itself , and give they some brief direction where to take 
 it , lift I out of it and into a hackney - coach he have choose from 
 the rest .

 CHAPTER LX 
 Perspective 
 I proceed to other passage of my narrative . from the goodness of 
 all about I I derive such consolation as I can never think of 
 unmoved . I have already say so much of myself , and so much still 
 remain , that I will not dwell upon my sorrow . I have an illness , 
 but it be not a long one ; and I would avoid even this mention of 
 it if I could quite keep down the recollection of their sympathy . 
 I proceed to other passage of my narrative . 
 during the time of my illness , we be still in London , where Mrs. 
 Woodcourt have come , on my guardian 's invitation , to stay with we . 
 when my guardian think I well and cheerful enough to talk with 
 he in our old way - though I could have do that soon if he

 he have appoint the time himself , and we be alone . 
 " Dame Trot , " say he , receive I with a kiss , " welcome to the 
 growlery again , my dear . I have a scheme to develop , little woman . 
 I propose to remain here , perhaps for six month , perhaps for a 
 long time - as it may be . quite to settle here for a while , in 
 short . " 
 " and in the meanwhile leave Bleak House ? " say I. 
 " Aye , my dear ? Bleak House , " he return , " must learn to take care 
 of itself . " 
 I think his tone sound sorrowful , but look at he , I see his 
 kind face light up by its pleasantest smile . 
 " Bleak House , " he repeat - and his tone do not sound sorrowful , I 
 find - " must learn to take care of itself . it be a long way from 
 Ada , my dear , and Ada stand much in need of you . "

 that virtue , since if you be generally on the road , you could be 
 seldom with I . and besides , I wish to hear as much and as often 
 of Ada as I can in this condition of estrangement from poor Rick . 
 not of she alone , but of he too , poor fellow . " 
 " have you see Mr. Woodcourt , this morning , guardian ? " 
 " I see Mr. Woodcourt every morning , Dame Durden . " 
 " do he still say the same of Richard ? " 
 " just the same . he know of no direct bodily illness that he have ; 
 on the contrary , he believe that he have none . yet he be not easy 
 about he ; who can be ? " 
 my dear girl have be to see we lately every day , some time twice 
 in a day . but we have foresee , all along , that this would only 
 last until I be quite myself . we know full well that her fervent 
 heart be as full of affection and gratitude towards her cousin

 CHAPTER LXI 
 A Discovery 
 the day when I frequent that miserable corner which my dear girl 
 brighten can never fade in my remembrance . I never see it , and I

 Esther 's Narrative

 CHAPTER LXVI 
 down in Lincolnshire 
 there be a hush upon Chesney Wold in these altered day , as there 
 be upon a portion of the family history . the story go that Sir 
 Leicester pay some who could have speak out to hold their peace ; 
 but it be a lame story , feebly whisper and creep about , and 
 any bright spark of life it show soon die away . it be know 
 for certain that the handsome Lady Dedlock lie in the mausoleum in 
 the park , where the tree arch darkly overhead , and the owl be 
 hear at night make the wood ring ; but whence she be bring 
 home to be lay among the echo of that solitary place , or how she 
 die , be all mystery . some of her old friend , principally to be 
 find among the peachy - cheek charmer with the skeleton throat , 
 do once occasionally say , as they toy in a ghastly manner with

 after lose all their other beaux - do once occasionally say , when

 up from among the fern in the hollow , and wind by the bridle- 
 road among the tree , come sometimes to this lonely spot the sound 
 of horse ' hoof . then may be see Sir Leicester - invalid , bent , 
 and almost blind , but of worthy presence yet - rid with a 
 stalwart man beside he , constant to his bridle - rein . when they 
 come to a certain spot before the mausoleum - door , Sir Leicester 's 
 accustomed horse stop of his own accord , and Sir Leicester , 
 pull off his hat , be still for a few moment before they ride 
 away . 
 War rage yet with the audacious Boythorn , though at uncertain 
 interval , and now hotly , and now coolly , flicker like an 
 unsteady fire . the truth be say to be that when Sir Leicester 
 come down to Lincolnshire for good , Mr. Boythorn show a manifest 
 desire to abandon his right of way and do whatever Sir Leicester 
 would , which Sir Leicester , conceive to be a condescension to his 
 illness or misfortune , take in such high dudgeon , and be so 
 magnificently aggrieve by , that Mr. Boythorn find himself under 
 the necessity of commit a flagrant trespass to restore his 
 neighbour to himself . similarly , Mr. Boythorn continue to post 
 tremendous placard on the dispute thoroughfare and   with his bird 
 upon his head   to hold forth vehemently against Sir Leicester in 
 the sanctuary of his own home ; similarly , also , he defy he as of 
 old in the little church by testify a bland unconsciousness of 
 his existence . but it be whisper that when he be most ferocious 
 towards his old foe , he be really most considerate , and that Sir 
 Leicester , in the dignity of be implacable , little suppose how 
 much he be humour . as little do he think how near together he 
 and his antagonist have suffer in the fortune of two sister , 
 and his antagonist , who know it now , be not the man to tell he . 
 so the quarrel go on to the satisfaction of both . 
 in one of the lodge of the park - that lodge within sight of the 
 house where , once upon a time , when the water be out down in 
 Lincolnshire , my Lady use to see the keeper 's child - the stalwart 
 man , the trooper formerly , be house . some relic of his old 
 calling hang upon the wall , and these it be the choose recreation 
 of a little lame man about the stable - yard to keep gleam bright .

 a goodly sight it be to see the grand old housekeeper   hard of 
 hear now   go to church on the arm of her son and to observe - 
 which few do , for the house be scant of company in these time - the 
 relation of both towards Sir Leicester , and his towards they . 
 they have visitor in the high summer weather , when a grey cloak 
 and umbrella , unknown to Chesney Wold at other period , be see 
 among the leaf ; when two young lady be occasionally find 
 gambolling in sequester saw - pit and such nook of the park ; and 
 when the smoke of two pipe wreathe away into the fragrant evening 
 air from the trooper 's door . then be a fife hear troll within

 while two man pace together up and down , " but I never own to it 
 before the old girl . Discipline must be maintain . " 
 the great part of the house be shut up , and it be a show - house no 
 long ; yet Sir Leicester hold his shrunken state in the long 
 drawing - room for all that , and repose in his old place before my 
 Lady 's picture . close in by night with broad screen , and 
 illumine only in that part , the light of the drawing - room seem

 Volumnia , grow with the flight of time pinker as to the red in 
 her face , and yellower as to the white , read to Sir Leicester in

 the cousin generally be rather shy of Chesney Wold in its 
 dullness , but take to it a little in the shooting season , when gun 
 be hear in the plantation , and a few scatter beater and 
 keeper wait at the old place of appointment for low - spirited two 
 and three of cousin . the debilitated cousin , more debilitate by

 hour and protest that such fernal old jail 's - nough t'sew fler 
 up - frever . 
 the only great occasion for Volumnia in this change aspect of the 
 place in Lincolnshire be those occasion , rare and widely 
 separate , when something be to be do for the county or the 
 country in the way of grace a public ball . then , indeed , do 
 the tuckered sylph come out in fairy form and proceed with joy 
 under cousinly escort to the exhausted old assembly - room , fourteen 
 heavy mile off , which , during three hundred and sixty - four day 
 and night of every ordinary year , be a kind of antipodean lumber- 
 room full of old chair and table upside down . then , indeed , do 
 she captivate all heart by her condescension , by her girlish 
 vivacity , and by she skip about as in the day when the hideous 
 old general with the mouth too full of tooth have not cut one of 
 they at two guineas each . then do she twirl and twine , a 
 pastoral nymph of good family , through the maze of the dance . 
 then do the swain appear with tea , with lemonade , with sandwich , 
 with homage . then be she kind and cruel , stately and unassuming , 
 various , beautifully wilful . then be there a singular kind of 
 parallel between she and the little glass chandelier of another 
 age embellish that assembly - room , which , with their meagre 
 stem , their spare little drop , their disappointing knob where no 
 drop be , their bare little stalk from which knob and drop have 
 both depart , and their little feeble prismatic twinkling , all 
 seem Volumnias . 
 for the rest , Lincolnshire life to Volumnia be a vast blank of 
 overgrown house look out upon tree , sighing , wring their 
 hand , bow their head , and cast their tear upon the window- 
 pane in monotonous depression . a labyrinth of grandeur , less the 
 property of an old family of human being and their ghostly 
 likeness than of an old family of echoing and thundering which 
 start out of their hundred graf at every sound and go resound 
 through the building . a waste of unused passage and staircase in 
 which to drop a comb upon a bedroom floor at night be to send a 
 stealthy footfall on an errand through the house . a place where 
 few people care to go about alone , where a maid scream if an ash 
 drop from the fire , take to cry at all time and season , 
 become the victim of a low disorder of the spirit , and give 
 warning and depart . 
 Thus Chesney Wold . with so much of itself abandon to darkness 
 and vacancy ; with so little change under the summer shin or the 
 wintry lowering ; so sombre and motionless always - no flag fly 
 now by day , no row of light sparkle by night ; with no family to 
 come and go , no visitor to be the soul of pale cold shape of 
 room , no stir of life about it - passion and pride , even to the 
 stranger 's eye , have die away from the place in Lincolnshire and 
 yield it to dull repose . 
 CHAPTER LXVII 
 the Close of Esther 's Narrative 
 full seven happy year I have be the mistress of Bleak House . 
 the few word that I have to add to what I have write be soon 
 pen ; then I and the unknown friend to whom I write will part for 
 ever . not without much dear remembrance on my side . not without 
 some , I hope , on his or her . 
 they give my darling into my arm , and through many week I never 
 leave she . the little child who be to have do so much be bear 
 before the turf be plant on its father 's grave . it be a boy ; 
 and I , my husband , and my guardian give he his father 's name . 
 the help that my dear count on do come to she , though it come , 
 in the eternal wisdom , for another purpose . though to bless and 
 restore his mother , not his father , be the errand of this baby , 
 its power be mighty to do it . when I see the strength of the weak 
 little hand and how its touch could heal my darling 's heart and 
 raise hope within she , I feel a new sense of the goodness and the 
 tenderness of God . 
 they throve , and by degree I see my dear girl pass into my country 
 garden and walk there with her infant in her arm . I be marry 
 then . I be the happy of the happy . 
 it be at this time that my guardian join we and ask Ada when 
 she would come home . 
 " both house be your home , my dear , " say he , " but the old Bleak 
 House claim priority . when you and my boy be strong enough to do 
 it , come and take possession of your home . " 
 Ada call he " her dear cousin , John . " but he say , no , it 
 must be guardian now . he be her guardian henceforth , and the 
 boy 's ; and he have an old association with the name . so she call 
 him guardian , and have call he guardian ever since . the child 
 know he by no other name . I say the child ; I have two little 
 daughter . 
 it be difficult to believe that Charley   round - eyed still , and not 
 at all grammatical   be married to a miller in our neighbourhood ; 
 yet so it be ; and even now , look up from my desk as I write 
 early in the morning at my summer window , I see the very mill 
 begin to go round . I hope the miller will not spoil Charley ; 
 but he be very fond of she , and Charley be rather vain of such a 
 match , for he be well to do and be in great request . so far as my 
 small maid be concern , I might suppose time to have stand for 
 seven year as still as the mill do half an hour ago , since little 
 Emma , Charley 's sister , be exactly what Charley use to be . as to 
 Tom , Charley 's brother , I be really afraid to say what he do at 
 school in cipher , but I think it be decimal . he be 
 apprentice to the miller , whatever it be , and be a good bashful 
 fellow , always fall in love with somebody and be ashamed of 
 it . 
 Caddy Jellyby pass she very last holiday with we and be a 
 dearer creature than ever , perpetually dance in and out of the 
 house with the child as if she have never give a dancing - lesson 
 in her life . Caddy keep she own little carriage now instead of 
 hire one , and live full two mile far westward than Newman 
 Street . she work very hard , her husband   an excellent one   be 
 lame and able to do very little . still , she be more than content 
 and do all she have to do with all her heart . Mr. Jellyby spend 
 his evening at her new house with his head against the wall as he 
 use to do in her old one . I have hear that Mrs. Jellyby be 
 understand to suffer great mortification from her daughter 's 
 ignoble marriage and pursuit , but I hope she get over it in time . 
 she have be disappoint in Borrioboola - Gha , which turn out a 
 failure in consequence of the king of Borrioboola want to sell 
 everybody - who survive the climate - for rum , but she have take up 
 with the right of woman to sit in Parliament , and Caddy tell I 
 it be a mission involve more correspondence than the old one . I 
 have almost forget Caddy 's poor little girl . she be not such a 
 mite now , but she be deaf and dumb . I believe there never be a 
 well mother than Caddy , who learn , in her scanty interval of 
 leisure , innumerable deaf and dumb art to soften the affliction of 
 her child . 
 as if I be never to have do with Caddy , I be remind here of 
 Peepy and old Mr. Turveydrop . Peepy be in the Custom House , and 
 do extremely well . old Mr. Turveydrop , very apoplectic , still 
 exhibit his deportment about town , still enjoy himself in the old 
 manner , be still believe in in the old way . he be constant in his 
 patronage of Peepy and be understand to have bequeath he a 
 favourite french clock in his dressing - room - which be not his 
 property . 
 with the first money we save at home , we add to our pretty house 
 by throw out a little growlery expressly for my guardian , which 
 we inaugurate with great splendour the next time he come down to 
 see we . I try to write all this lightly , because my heart be full 
 in draw to an end , but when I write of he , my tear will have 
 their way . 
 I never look at he but I hear our poor dear Richard call he a 
 good man . to Ada and her pretty boy , he be the fond father ; to 
 I he be what he have ever be , and what name can I give to that ? 
 he be my husband 's good and dear friend , he be our child 's 
 darling , he be the object of our deep love and veneration . yet 
 while I feel towards he as if he be a superior being , I be so 
 familiar with he and so easy with he that I almost wonder at 
 myself . I have never lose my old name , nor have he lose his ; nor 
 do I ever , when he be with we , sit in any other place than in my 
 old chair at his side , Dame Trot , Dame Durden , Little Woman - all 
 just the same as ever ; and I answer , " yes , dear guardian ! " just the 
 same . 
 I have never know the wind to be in the east for a single moment 
 since the day when he take I to the porch to read the name . I 
 remark to he once that the wind seem never in the east now , 
 and he say , no , truly ; it have finally depart from that quarter 
 on that very day . 
 I think my darling girl be more beautiful than ever . the sorrow 
 that have be in her face - for it be not there now - seem to have 
 purify even its innocent expression and to have give it a 
 diviner quality . sometimes when I raise my eye and see she in the 
 black dress that she still wear , teach my Richard , I feel - it 
 be difficult to express - as if it be so good to know that she 
 remember her dear Esther in her prayer . 
 I call he my Richard ! but he say that he have two mama , and I be 
 one . 
 we be not rich in the bank , but we have always prosper , and we 
 have quite enough . I never walk out with my husband but I hear the 
 people bless he . I never go into a house of any degree but I hear 
 his praise or see they in grateful eye . I never lie down at 
 night but I know that in the course of that day he have alleviate 
 pain and soothe some fellow - creature in the time of need . I know 
 that from the bed of those who be past recovery , thank have 
 often , often go up , in the last hour , for his patient 
 ministration . be not this to be rich ? 
 the people even praise I as the doctor 's wife . the people even 
 like I as I go about , and make so much of I that I be quite 
 abash . I owe it all to he , my love , my pride ! they like I for 
 his sake , as I do everything I do in life for his sake . 
 a night or two ago , after bustle about prepare for my darling 
 and my guardian and little Richard , who be come to - morrow , I be 
 sit out in the porch of all place , that dearly memorable 
 porch , when Allan come home . so he say , " my precious little 
 woman , what be you do here ? " and I say , " the moon be shin 
 so brightly , Allan , and the night be so delicious , that I have be 
 sit here think . " 
 " what have you be think about , my dear ? " say Allan then . 
 " how curious you be ! " say I. " I be almost ashamed to tell you , 
 but I will . I have be think about my old look - such as they 
 be . " 
 " and what have you be think about THEM , my busy bee ? " say 
 Allan . 
 " I have be think that I think it be impossible that you 
 could have love I any well , even if I have retain they . " 
 " ' such as they be ' ? " say Allan , laugh . 
 " such as they be , of course . " 
 " my dear Dame Durden , " say Allan , draw my arm through his , " do 
 you ever look in the glass ? " 
 " you know I do ; you see I do it . " 
 " and do not   you know that you be pretty than you ever be ? " 
 " I do not know that ; I be not certain that I know it now . but I 
 know that my dear little pet be very pretty , and that my 
 darling be very beautiful , and that my husband be very handsome , 
 and that my guardian have the bright and most benevolent face 
 that ever be see , and that they can very well do without much 
 beauty in I - even supposing - . "
